title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: 3D Object Tracking in Sparse Point Clouds

## Summary

This paper presents a novel approach to 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds, combining shape-aware feature learning with a voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The work is clearly written, technically sound, and demonstrates strong experimental results across multiple benchmarks. However, certain claims regarding the method's superior handling of sparse point clouds require more rigorous justification, and the ablation studies need to be more comprehensive.

## Strengths

The paper addresses a practically important problem in autonomous driving and 3D perception, where sparse point cloud data is increasingly common. The writing is clear and well-organized, with sufficient technical details to understand the methodology. The authors provide good motivation for their design choices, including the use of both global and local feature embeddings and the voxel-to-BEV conversion strategy.

The experimental results are impressive, showing substantial improvements over previous methods on both KITTI and nuScenes datasets. The generalization across multiple datasets is particularly noteworthy and demonstrates the robustness of the approach. Additionally, the method is novel with clearly articulated differences from prior work, and the related work section adequately positions this contribution within the broader literature.

## Weaknesses

Despite the overall strong results, there are several concerns that limit the contribution:

**1. Unsubstantiated Claims About Sparse Point Cloud Handling**

The central claim that the method is better suited for sparse point clouds is not convincingly supported. A careful comparison between Tables 1 and 2 reveals a problematic pattern: baseline methods (SC3D and P2B) actually demonstrate better robustness when transitioning from dense to sparse scenarios, while the proposed method suffers a noticeable performance drop. This observation directly contradicts the paper's core argument and requires explicit explanation.

**2. Incomplete Ablation Studies**

The ablation studies in Table 3 only evaluate the car category, which is insufficient for validating design choices across diverse object types. The paper lacks ablation studies justifying the necessity of both global and local features in either the template feature embedding module or the shape-aware feature learning module. Without these studies, it remains unclear whether the added complexity contributes meaningfully to performance.

**3. Unclear Connection Between Components and Sparse Point Cloud Performance**

While the shape-aware feature learning module generates dense point clouds using Chamfer distance loss, the paper does not adequately explain why this approach specifically benefits sparse point cloud tracking. The mechanism by which this design choice addresses sparsity challenges needs clearer exposition.

**4. Limited Discussion of Method Limitations**

The paper does not adequately discuss scenarios where the method may struggle. For instance, the reliance on BEV representations raises questions about applicability to indoor scenes where bird's-eye-view projections may not be suitable or informative.

**5. Imprecise Language**

Some statements appear awkwardly phrased or imprecise, which detracts from the technical clarity. These should be refined for publication.

## Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen this work, I recommend:

1. **Expand Ablation Studies**: Conduct comprehensive ablation studies across all four object categories (car, pedestrian, van, cyclist) rather than only the car category. This will make claims about the necessity of different components more convincing.

2. **Rigorously Analyze Sparse Point Cloud Performance**: Provide deeper analysis explaining why the method performs differently than baselines in sparse versus dense scenarios. Address the apparent contradiction shown in Tables 1 and 2 directly.

3. **Justify Architectural Choices**: Add explicit ablation studies demonstrating the necessity of both global and local features in both the template feature embedding and shape-aware feature learning modules.

4. **Clarify Language**: Review the manuscript for imprecise or awkward phrasing to ensure all claims are stated with precision.

5. **Discuss Limitations**: Add a dedicated section discussing the method's limitations, particularly regarding applicability to indoor scenes and other scenarios where BEV representations may be insufficient.

## Minor Comments

The technical approach is sound, with reasonable choices such as the 0.3-meter voxel size and the use of PointNet++ as backbone. The three detection heads (2D-center, offset & rotation, z-axis) are well-motivated.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid contribution to 3D object tracking with impressive experimental results and clear presentation. The approach is technically sound and demonstrates strong generalization across datasets. However, the work would be significantly strengthened by addressing the gaps in experimental justification, particularly regarding claims about sparse point cloud handling, and by providing more comprehensive ablation studies. These issues prevent the paper from being a strong accept, but the overall quality and empirical results justify acceptance.

**Recommendation: Accept**

The paper merits publication despite its limitations, as it presents a novel, technically sound method with impressive experimental results. The identified weaknesses should be addressed in revision to enhance the contribution's clarity and rigor.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.
reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: 3D Object Tracking with Point Cloud Generation

## Summary

This paper presents a 3D object tracker that achieves competitive empirical results on standard benchmarks. While the work demonstrates solid experimental performance and includes useful computational cost analysis, it suffers from significant presentation issues, insufficient justification of design complexity, and limited novelty in key components. The paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold but requires substantial revisions to address these concerns.

## Strengths

The paper makes a convincing empirical contribution, with the proposed method clearly outperforming prior state-of-the-art approaches by noticeable margins. The authors have properly established baselines and report improved performance metrics that support their claims. The inclusion of computational cost analysis is commendable and demonstrates awareness of real-world applicability concerns.

The experimental validation includes ablation studies that provide support for the proposed components. Additionally, the paper presents an interesting empirical finding: the auxiliary point cloud generation task, used only during training, enhances feature expressiveness for the localization task. This observation suggests thoughtful experimental design and provides useful insights for the community.

## Major Weaknesses

**Writing Quality**: The paper exhibits numerous grammatical errors and unpolished prose that detract from its technical content. Before publication, the manuscript requires thorough proofreading and revision to meet the standards expected for a top-tier venue.

**Insufficient Ablation Studies**: The Template Feature Embedding Network is architecturally complex, combining global and local feature branches with multiple distance functions for correlation and similarity maps. However, the ablation studies provided are insufficient to justify this complexity. Critical ablations are missing, including: (1) isolated analysis of global versus local feature contributions, (2) comparison of different distance functions employed in the correlation and similarity computations, and (3) comparison against simpler architectural alternatives. Given the conference venue's expectations, such comprehensive ablations are essential for validating design choices.

**Limited Novelty and Attribution**: The Voxel-to-BEV target localization network appears heavily inspired by established 3D object detection methods, particularly PointPillar and SECOND. The paper fails to cite these important related works or adequately discuss how its approach relates to and differs from these prior designs. This represents a significant oversight that must be addressed. The contribution of this component to the tracking task specifically remains unclear.

**Missing Analysis of Limitations**: The paper lacks thorough discussion of limitations and societal impact, which is expected for work at this level. Additionally, the failure case analysis is limited. The paper does not adequately address tracking-specific challenges such as re-identification of objects and tracklet initialization, which are distinct from object detection and warrant dedicated discussion.

## Technical Concerns

The point cloud generation auxiliary task is an interesting design choice, but its role deserves deeper analysis. Why does a task discarded at test time improve feature learning? The paper would benefit from more rigorous investigation of this phenomenon beyond the empirical observation that it works.

The shape generation network employs a global-local branch architecture, but no comparison to simpler alternatives is provided. This raises questions about whether the added complexity is truly necessary.

## Minor Suggestions

1. Conduct comprehensive proofreading to eliminate grammatical errors and improve overall writing quality.

2. Provide detailed ablation studies for the Template Feature Embedding Network, including isolated analysis of global/local features and systematic comparison of distance functions.

3. Properly cite PointPillar and SECOND, and discuss the relationship between your Voxel-to-BEV localization module and these existing 3D detection approaches.

4. Expand the limitations section to include discussion of tracking-specific challenges (re-identification, tracklet initialization) and societal impact.

5. Include more comprehensive failure case analysis to characterize when and why the method struggles.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents solid empirical results and includes useful practical analysis. However, it is held back by presentation quality issues, insufficient ablation studies for the proposed complexity, and limited novelty in key components without proper attribution to related work. The work is marginally above the acceptance threshold—the strong experimental results and interesting empirical findings support acceptance, but only with the expectation that authors will address the writing quality and provide more rigorous justification of their design choices. The paper would be significantly strengthened by more comprehensive ablations and clearer positioning relative to existing methods.

**Recommendation: Accept (marginally)**
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for 3D Object Tracking

## Summary

This paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds, with evaluation on the KITTI dataset. The work addresses a relevant problem in autonomous driving and demonstrates substantial empirical improvements over existing baselines. However, concerns about generalization, robustness across object categories, and limited scope of evaluation prevent this from being a strong contribution.

## Strengths

1. **Well-motivated approach**: The paper tackles an important and challenging problem in autonomous driving with a sensible algorithmic design that effectively leverages voxel and bird's-eye-view representations.

2. **Strong empirical results on KITTI**: The method achieves significant performance improvements over existing approaches on the KITTI benchmark, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed design.

3. **Clear presentation**: The paper is generally well-written with helpful visualizations that effectively communicate the design intuitions and motivate the technical choices.

4. **Code availability**: The authors' commitment to release code as supplementary material is commendable and will benefit the research community.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Category-dependent performance degradation**: The substantial performance gap in the cyclist category (30.4/37.2 on sparse scenarios vs. 64.7/77.4 for cars) raises serious questions about the robustness and generalizability of the approach. The paper provides insufficient analysis of why this degradation occurs and whether similar issues would emerge with other categories under limited training data conditions.

2. **Limited evaluation scope**: Evaluation is restricted to the KITTI dataset without cross-dataset validation on other benchmarks (e.g., nuScenes). This is a significant limitation for a method targeting safety-critical autonomous driving applications, where generalization to different sensors, environments, and driving conditions is crucial.

3. **Hyperparameter tuning and domain adaptation**: The method requires tuning multiple hyperparameters (loss coefficients λ₁, λ₂, λ₃ and voxel size parameters) but provides limited guidance on how to adjust these when adapting to new datasets. This substantially limits the practical applicability of the work.

4. **Insufficient justification for sensor modality**: For safety-critical autonomous driving, the paper does not adequately justify why additional RGB information is not leveraged alongside LiDAR data. This omission is not thoroughly discussed despite being relevant to real-world deployment.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Figure 3 clarity**: The color scheme in Figure 3 lacks clear semantic correspondence to the diagram sections, making it difficult to parse the visualization effectively.

2. **Limited qualitative analysis**: While supplementary visualizations are mentioned, the paper would benefit from video demonstrations of tracking outputs in the supplementary materials to provide more intuitive understanding of the method's behavior.

3. **Generalization to real-world scenarios**: The paper does not discuss how the algorithm would perform under diverse real-world conditions (weather variations, different lighting, sensor degradation, etc.) that differ from KITTI.

## Technical Concerns

- The significant variation in voxel size sensitivity (0.1-0.4 meters) suggests dataset-specific tuning is required, which is problematic for generalization.
- The category-specific performance variation suggests the method may not be robust to data distribution shifts or underrepresented categories.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Provide deeper analysis of the cyclist performance degradation and discuss whether this is an inherent limitation of the approach or a data-driven issue.

2. Include video demonstrations of tracking results in supplementary materials for better visualization of the method's qualitative behavior.

3. Redesign Figure 3 with clearer color semantics or alternative visualization approaches.

4. Evaluate on additional datasets (nuScenes, Waymo, or other LiDAR-based benchmarks) to demonstrate generalization capability.

5. Provide clearer guidelines or ablation studies on hyperparameter selection for new domains and datasets.

6. Discuss the potential for incorporating RGB information and justify the LiDAR-only choice for autonomous driving applications.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a technically sound approach with strong empirical results on KITTI. However, the evaluation is limited in scope, generalization to new domains is not thoroughly explored, and significant performance gaps across object categories raise concerns about robustness. While the work makes a contribution to 3D object tracking, these limitations prevent it from being a strong accept. The paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold—it presents sufficient technical merit and experimental validation on a standard benchmark to warrant publication, but the concerns about generalization, category-dependent performance, and limited evaluation scope should be addressed in future work to strengthen the contribution.

**Recommendation: Accept (marginally above threshold)**

The work merits publication as a solid contribution to the field, but authors should prioritize addressing generalization concerns and providing more comprehensive evaluation in future iterations.

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for 3D Single Object Tracking

## Summary

This paper presents a Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker designed for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The approach converts sparse point cloud data into a dense bird's-eye-view representation and employs a two-step tracking framework combining region proposal generation with dense feature matching. While the work demonstrates solid experimental improvements on the KITTI dataset and shows reasonable generalization to nuScenes, significant concerns exist regarding the clarity of technical novelty, presentation quality, and completeness of the experimental evaluation.

## Strengths

1. **Strong Empirical Results**: The paper shows notable quantitative improvements over state-of-the-art methods on the KITTI dataset across multiple object categories, with good generalization demonstrated on the nuScenes dataset.

2. **Well-Motivated Design Choice**: The conversion of sparse point clouds to dense BEV representations is a sensible approach to address the challenges of sparse data, and the overall strategy is intuitive.

3. **Comprehensive Quantitative Evaluation**: The paper provides extensive quantitative results across multiple metrics and object categories, demonstrating the method's effectiveness in various tracking scenarios.

4. **Practical Architecture**: The use of template feature embeddings with max pooling and MLPs to capture global shape information, combined with gate mechanisms for feature enhancement, represents a reasonable design for the tracking task.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Insufficient Differentiation from Prior Work**: The paper's relationship to closely related methods P2B and SC3D is inadequately explained. The two-step framework largely follows P2B's structure, while the shape-aware feature learning module appears to be a minor modification of SC3D's shape completion regularization loss. The paper fails to clearly delineate what is inherited versus what constitutes novel contribution, making it difficult to assess the true technical novelty.

2. **Poor Method Presentation**: The method section is dense with technical details but lacks sufficient context and motivation for architectural choices. Why is the voxel size of 0.3 meters optimal? What design principles guided the specific head configurations for localization? These questions remain unanswered, making the work difficult to follow and reproduce.

3. **Incomplete Experimental Evaluation**: 
   - No ablation study specifically examining the modifications made to SC3D's shape completion loss
   - Missing qualitative comparison results with both SC3D and P2B across all object categories (only Table 5 provides limited qualitative results)
   - Inconsistent performance on the cyclist category compared to SC3D is not adequately explained
   - Unexplained discrepancy in speed metrics: the paper reports 17 FPS on TITAN RTX, yet the official P2B work reports 40 FPS on a 1080Ti, raising questions about experimental setup and fair comparison

4. **Missing Critical Discussions**: The paper lacks any discussion of method limitations or potential negative societal impacts, which are increasingly expected in modern academic work.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. The shape generation network is only used during training and discarded at inference, which is reasonable for efficiency but should be more explicitly highlighted in the main text.

2. The Chamfer distance loss for shape generation is mentioned but not thoroughly justified relative to alternative loss functions.

## Specific Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Clarify Technical Novelty**: Add a detailed table or section explicitly comparing your approach to P2B and SC3D, clearly identifying which components are inherited and which are novel modifications. This is essential for understanding the paper's contribution.

2. **Strengthen Method Presentation**: Reorganize the method section to provide better motivation for each design choice. Include ablation studies that justify key decisions (e.g., voxel size selection, head configurations).

3. **Add Targeted Ablation Study**: Provide a specific ablation study examining the modifications made to SC3D's shape completion regularization loss to justify the technical novelty of this component.

4. **Expand Qualitative Comparisons**: Beyond Table 5, include visual qualitative comparisons with SC3D and P2B across all four object categories to demonstrate where and why your method improves upon prior work.

5. **Address Cyclist Performance**: Provide a detailed analysis of why performance on the cyclist category lags behind SC3D. Include visualizations or error analysis to explain this discrepancy.

6. **Clarify Speed Metrics**: Provide detailed experimental setup information for the speed comparisons and explain the discrepancy with P2B's reported FPS. Ensure fair comparison conditions.

7. **Add Limitations and Impact Discussion**: Include a brief section discussing the method's limitations (e.g., sensitivity to sparse regions, failure cases) and a statement on potential societal impacts.

## Questions for Authors

- How much of the improvement comes from the BEV representation versus the specific architectural modifications to prior work?
- Why does the method underperform on cyclists compared to SC3D?
- Can you provide the exact experimental setup used for speed measurements?

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a competent approach to 3D object tracking with encouraging experimental results. However, it suffers from presentation issues and incomplete experimental analysis that obscure its true technical contributions. The work appears to be an incremental improvement over P2B and SC3D, but the paper does not make this relationship sufficiently clear. With revisions addressing the clarity of novelty, method motivation, and more comprehensive experimental comparisons, this work could make a solid contribution to the field. In its current form, it is marginally above the acceptance threshold—the strong empirical results justify acceptance, but significant revisions would strengthen the contribution considerably.

**Recommendation: Accept with Major Revisions**

The paper merits publication based on its experimental improvements, but the authors must substantially improve the presentation of technical novelty, provide more comprehensive ablation studies, and address the inconsistencies in experimental reporting before final acceptance.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This paper presents a 3D object detection method that combines template matching, shape completion, and bird's-eye-view (BEV) localization for autonomous driving applications. While the experimental results are encouraging, the work suffers from a fundamental limitation in its novelty, as it primarily assembles existing techniques without introducing sufficiently original contributions. The paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, with strong empirical performance offset by concerns regarding the distinctiveness of the technical approach.

## Strengths

1. **Strong Experimental Results**: The method demonstrates promising performance on two major benchmarks (KITTI and nuScenes), indicating that the assembled pipeline is effective in practice.

2. **Comprehensive Evaluation**: The experimental validation on established datasets provides confidence in the practical utility of the approach.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Limited Technical Novelty**: The core issue with this work is that it combines well-established components without introducing meaningful novel contributions. Each major component appears to be borrowed from existing literature with minimal modification or innovation.

2. **Lack of Differentiation from Prior Work**: 
   - The template matching approach closely mirrors the SiamRPN framework without clear justification for how this implementation differs or improves upon it.
   - The shape-completion network follows conventional shape completion techniques without novel architectural or methodological advances.
   - The BEV localization strategy is substantially similar to LaserNet's approach, raising questions about the original contribution in this module.

3. **Insufficient Ablation Studies**: The paper critically lacks comprehensive ablation studies to validate the individual contributions of its components. Specifically, there are no ablation studies examining the global and local template feature components, making it difficult to assess which elements actually contribute to the final performance and whether each component justifies its inclusion.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Unclear Novelty Justification**: The paper does not adequately articulate what distinguishes this work from prior methods or why combining these existing approaches constitutes a sufficient contribution to warrant publication.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Conduct Detailed Ablation Studies**: Provide comprehensive ablation experiments isolating the contributions of global and local template features, as well as other key modules. This would clarify which components are essential and what value each adds to the overall system.

2. **Strengthen Novelty Arguments**: Provide more rigorous analysis and justification of how the proposed method differs from SiamRPN and LaserNet. Clearly articulate the novel design choices, if any, and explain why these modifications are necessary and beneficial.

3. **Deeper Technical Analysis**: Offer more detailed comparisons with existing approaches, highlighting specific improvements in architecture, training strategy, or inference methodology that distinguish this work from prior art.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a competent engineering effort that achieves good empirical results by combining existing techniques. However, the limited novelty and lack of thorough ablation studies are significant concerns. The work reads more as an application or integration study rather than a methodological contribution with sufficient originality for a strong acceptance. The strong experimental performance prevents outright rejection, but the work remains marginally above the acceptance threshold. To strengthen this contribution, the authors should focus on either identifying and highlighting genuine novel components or providing extensive ablation studies that justify the value of each assembled module.

**Recommendation: Accept (marginally above threshold)**

The results  look promising.

My major concern if novelty is limited.  The proposed method is an ensemble of well-known components: template matching (eg. SiamRPN[1]), shape-completion and bev localization (e.g. LaserNet[2]).

Ablation studies of global and local template features are desired.

[1]Li, Bo, et al. "High performance visual tracking with siamese region proposal network." Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2018.
[2]Meyer, Gregory P., et al. "Lasernet: An efficient probabilistic 3d object detector for autonomous driving." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2019.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Point Cloud-Based 3D Single-Object Tracking with Shape Completion and Voxel-to-BEV Localization

## Summary

This paper presents a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking approach designed to handle sparse point cloud scenarios. The method incorporates shape completion on the search area and employs a voxel-to-BEV localization strategy. While the work demonstrates engineering effort and achieves performance improvements, the novelty is limited and several key design decisions lack adequate justification and experimental validation.

## Strengths

1. **Well-motivated feature extraction design**: The local and global feature embedding approach for template feature extraction is intuitive and grounded in sound reasoning. This design choice effectively captures both fine-grained and contextual information from the template.

2. **Strong empirical validation of template features**: The ablation experiments in Table 3 convincingly demonstrate that the proposed template feature embedding significantly contributes to performance improvements, validating this component of the approach.

3. **Effective qualitative demonstration**: The paper provides compelling qualitative analysis that clearly illustrates the method's utility in handling sparse point cloud scenarios, making the practical motivation evident.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Insufficient Justification for Shape Completion Location

The paper proposes shape completion on the search area but does not adequately justify why this is superior to shape completion on the template, as employed in prior work [21]. Critically, no direct experimental comparison between these two approaches is provided within the same codebase. This represents a fundamental design choice that requires rigorous validation. The intuition behind which approach is superior remains unclear, and the question of whether combining both approaches might yield better results is entirely unaddressed.

### 2. Problematic Voxel-to-BEV Design and Lack of Ablation

The voxel-to-BEV localization component raises significant concerns. The max-pooling operation along the z-axis appears to discard critical information, which should logically make z-axis center estimation more difficult, not easier. The paper claims this design improves z-axis localization, but this counterintuitive claim lacks clear theoretical or empirical justification. Furthermore, the paper provides no detailed ablation analysis isolating the contributions of voxel-to-BEV to 2D center estimation versus z-axis center estimation separately. Without such analysis, it is difficult to assess whether this component genuinely improves localization or merely adds computational overhead.

### 3. Unjustified Architectural Choices

The selection of PointNet++ (2017) as the backbone is not justified. Given the rapid advancement in point cloud processing methods over the past six years, the paper should either provide clear reasoning for this choice or upgrade to more recent architectures. This decision appears dated and potentially limits the method's effectiveness.

### 4. Severely Limited Evaluation Protocol

The evaluation is restricted to only two KITTI test sequences (scenes 19-20), which is problematic for several reasons:
- The KITTI tracking dataset is inherently small, making two sequences an insufficient testbed
- Certain object categories are severely underrepresented: Van appears in only 1,248 frames and Cyclist in merely 308 frames
- Results on these categories are likely subject to high variance and may not be statistically reliable
- This evaluation protocol is substantially weaker than contemporary benchmarks like nuScenes (150+ validation and test sequences) or Waymo Open Dataset

While nuScenes evaluation is included, it is limited to the validation set with key frames only, not the full test set.

## Minor Weaknesses

- The paper would benefit from clearer exposition of the technical mechanisms underlying the voxel-to-BEV approach
- The choice of Chamfer distance loss for shape completion training is not compared against alternative loss formulations
- The three prediction heads (2D-center, offset & rotation, z-axis) lack individual performance analysis

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Conduct direct comparisons**: Implement both shape completion strategies (template vs. search area) within the same experimental framework to fairly evaluate their relative merits.

2. **Explore combined approaches**: Investigate whether applying shape completion to both template and search area yields synergistic benefits.

3. **Provide detailed ablations**: Decompose the voxel-to-BEV component's contribution by separately measuring its impact on 2D center and z-axis center estimation.

4. **Justify design choices**: Provide either theoretical analysis or empirical evidence explaining why max-pooling along the z-axis benefits z-axis localization.

5. **Modernize architecture**: Either justify the use of PointNet++ or upgrade to more contemporary point cloud processing methods.

6. **Expand evaluation**: Migrate evaluation to larger, more comprehensive benchmarks such as nuScenes (full test set) or Waymo Open Dataset to enable more robust conclusions.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a competent engineering effort with incremental improvements to point cloud-based tracking. The template feature embedding component is well-designed and properly validated. However, the work suffers from insufficient justification of key design decisions, particularly regarding shape completion location and voxel-to-BEV localization. The evaluation protocol, while achieving positive results, is too limited to support strong claims about the method's effectiveness. The restriction to two KITTI sequences is particularly concerning given the sparsity of certain object categories. The paper would be significantly strengthened by direct experimental comparisons of design alternatives, clearer theoretical justification, and evaluation on more comprehensive benchmarks.

Despite these limitations, the work demonstrates sufficient technical quality and empirical improvements to merit publication, though substantial revisions addressing the evaluation scope and design justifications would substantially improve its contribution.

**Recommendation: Accept (6 - Marginally above the acceptance threshold)**

Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
