reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for 3D Object Tracking

## Summary

This paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-BEV architecture for 3D object tracking in point clouds and demonstrates competitive results on the KITTI dataset. While the paper is well-written and technically sound, it suffers from significant conceptual and experimental limitations that prevent it from making a sufficient contribution for acceptance at a top-tier venue.

## Strengths

1. **Clear Presentation**: The paper is well-organized and easy to follow. The technical approach is presented clearly, making it accessible to readers.

2. **Strong KITTI Results**: The method achieves competitive performance on the KITTI tracking benchmark, with meaningful improvements demonstrated through ablation studies.

3. **Reasonable Technical Design**: The Siamese voxel-to-BEV architecture is a sensible approach for 3D tracking tasks, and the engineering is competent.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Fundamental Conceptual Confusion

The paper presents itself as a tracking framework but fundamentally appears to be a detection method. Critical tracking components are absent or poorly explained:
- How are tracks initiated and terminated?
- How is occlusion handling addressed?
- What mechanisms ensure temporal consistency across frames?

The paper does not clearly articulate how it extends beyond single-frame detection to constitute a true tracking pipeline. This ambiguity raises questions about whether the method is actually suited for single object tracking (SOT) or multiple object tracking (MOT), and this distinction is never explicitly clarified.

### 2. Insufficient and Narrow Related Work

The related work section cherry-picks references and omits substantial portions of the 3D multiple object tracking literature. Notable gaps include:
- Missing recent 3D MOT methods from IROS 2020, ICRA 2021, and other major venues
- No coverage of important benchmarks (nuScenes, Waymo, JRDB) despite mentioning them
- Incomplete representation of the state-of-the-art in 3D tracking

A comprehensive tracking paper should engage thoroughly with existing tracking methodologies across multiple datasets and communities.

### 3. Inadequate Experimental Validation

The experimental evaluation has several critical limitations:
- **Non-standard Metrics**: Uses Success/Precision instead of standard KITTI tracking metrics (MOTA, HOTA)
- **Training Set Only**: Evaluates on KITTI training set rather than the official test set
- **Single Dataset**: Relies exclusively on KITTI despite claiming broader applicability
- **Limited Scope**: nuScenes evaluation only on validation set key frames, not comprehensive evaluation
- **No Leaderboard Validation**: Does not compare against official KITTI leaderboards

For an application-focused paper, such limited experimental scope is insufficient. The use of non-standard metrics further complicates comparison with existing tracking methods.

### 4. Lack of Theoretical Contribution

The paper presents an engineering application of existing machine learning techniques with minor architectural modifications. There is no novel theoretical insight, methodological innovation, or principled approach to the tracking problem. For a venue expecting research contributions, this is a significant limitation.

### 5. Generalization Concerns

- **Non-rigid Objects**: The method's applicability to non-rigid objects (e.g., humans) is unclear. How are templates defined for such objects? How does the Siamese approach handle deformable objects?
- **Missing Evaluations**: Despite claiming general applicability, the paper does not evaluate on 3D human tracking datasets (e.g., JRDB) or comprehensively on nuScenes and Waymo.
- **Limited Evidence**: The evidence for generalization beyond vehicle tracking on KITTI is insufficient.

### 6. Missing Critical Discussions

The paper lacks:
- **Limitations Section**: No honest discussion of failure cases, dataset biases, or method constraints
- **Societal Impact**: No consideration of ethical implications, particularly regarding privacy concerns in surveillance and tracking applications

## Minor Weaknesses

- Template definition strategy for handling different object categories is not explained
- No detailed comparison with 3D MOT baselines from the recent literature
- Ablation studies could be more comprehensive, comparing against more baseline variants

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Clarify the Scope**: Explicitly state whether this is SOT or MOT, and clearly articulate how tracking components are addressed.

2. **Strengthen Related Work**: Provide comprehensive coverage of 3D MOT literature across KITTI, nuScenes, Waymo, and JRDB benchmarks. Engage with recent tracking methods from major venues.

3. **Comprehensive Experimental Evaluation**:
   - Evaluate on official KITTI test set using standard KITTI tracking metrics (MOTA, HOTA)
   - Extend evaluation to nuScenes and Waymo with standard metrics
   - Compare directly against state-of-the-art 3D MOT methods
   - Test on JRDB or similar human tracking datasets if claiming generality

4. **Address Tracking Mechanisms**: Provide explicit explanations and implementation details for track initiation, termination, and occlusion handling.

5. **Template Handling**: Clarify how templates are defined and managed for different object types, especially non-rigid objects.

6. **Add Discussions**: Include sections on limitations and societal impact, particularly addressing privacy concerns.

## Recommendation

While this paper presents a competent engineering effort with reasonable results on KITTI, it falls short of the standards expected for a top-tier venue. The fundamental confusion about whether this is a tracking or detection method, combined with narrow related work coverage, limited experimental validation using non-standard metrics, and lack of theoretical contribution, collectively make this work insufficient for acceptance. The paper would benefit from substantial revision to clarify its scope, significantly expand experimental evaluation across multiple benchmarks with standard metrics, and provide deeper engagement with the tracking literature.

**Decision: Reject (4 - Ok but not good enough)**
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. Main Review:

In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Improved Generalization Analysis of MAML

## Summary

This paper presents a theoretical analysis of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) with improved generalization bounds. The authors introduce a new stability notion—(γ, K)-uniform stability—that explicitly captures dependence on both the number of tasks and samples per task. The work analyzes generalization in two settings: when the test task belongs to the training task distribution and when it is drawn from a different distribution. The analysis employs total variation distance to characterize generalization to unseen tasks.

## Strengths

1. **Novel Stability Framework**: The introduction of (γ, K)-uniform stability is well-motivated and represents a meaningful contribution. By explicitly tracking changes in both the inner-loop data (K points in S_i^in) and outer-loop data (1 point in S_i^out), the definition naturally reflects MAML's structure and enables more nuanced analysis than prior work.

2. **Improved Bounds**: The paper achieves a generalization error bound of O(1/mn), which is a notable improvement over prior results of O(1/√m) or O(1/m). This improvement arises from the ability to separately account for the number of tasks m and samples per task n, which is theoretically significant.

3. **Comprehensive Analysis**: The separation of training error and generalization error addresses a genuine gap in prior work that implicitly assumed fresh samples at each iteration. This more realistic treatment strengthens the theoretical contribution.

4. **Novel Approach to Unseen Tasks**: The use of total variation distance to bound the difference between performance on unseen tasks and training tasks is creative and provides a principled way to characterize robustness to task distribution shift.

5. **Presentation Quality**: The paper is generally well-structured and clearly written, with appropriate use of remarks to acknowledge limitations (e.g., Remark 2 on convexity assumptions).

## Weaknesses

1. **Restrictive Convexity Assumption**: The restriction to strongly convex loss functions is a significant limitation that severely restricts practical applicability. Modern MAML implementations predominantly use deep neural networks with non-convex objectives. The paper acknowledges this limitation but does not adequately discuss the gap between theory and practice or provide intuition about whether insights might transfer to non-convex settings.

2. **Single-Step Adaptation Analysis**: The theoretical analysis only covers single-step SGD adaptation at test time, which is substantially more limited than the multi-step adaptation commonly employed in practical MAML implementations. The paper does not discuss the challenges or implications of extending the analysis to K > 1 adaptation steps, which is a critical omission.

3. **Unclear Practical Implications**: Beyond the theoretical bounds themselves, the paper does not sufficiently discuss what insights these results provide for practitioners or how they might guide algorithm design and hyperparameter selection for real applications.

4. **Technical Presentation Issues**: Several minor but distracting errors are present:
   - Typo in Assumption 1(ii): "unbounded" requires correction
   - Citation error on Line 196: verify whether reference should be [24] instead of [1]
   - Typo on Line 218: "deferentially private" should be "differentially private"
   - Notation inconsistency on Lines 286-287: 'k' should be 'K'
   - Inconsistent capitalization in Section 3.3 title

## Technical Soundness and Main Contributions

The theoretical development appears sound. The key results are:

- **Definition 3** introduces (γ, K)-uniform stability with γ = O(1)G²(1+αLK)/(mnμ), appropriately capturing the algorithm's dependence structure.
- **Theorem 1** establishes the connection between this stability notion and generalization error for symmetric algorithms.
- **Theorem 2** proves MAML satisfies (γ, K)-uniform stability, with the bound depending on standard smoothness and strong convexity parameters.
- **Theorem 3** bounds generalization to unseen tasks via total variation distance, employing a maximal coupling technique to eliminate dependence on K.
- **Proposition 2** combines training and generalization error bounds to characterize excess population loss.

The analysis appropriately requires strong convexity parameter μ, smoothness L, Hessian Lipschitz continuity ρ, and bounded gradients G. The non-atomic distribution assumption (Assumption 2) is reasonable and well-justified.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Multi-Step Adaptation**: Provide at least a discussion of the challenges in extending the analysis to K > 1 adaptation steps. Even a high-level treatment of obstacles would strengthen the work.

2. **Non-Convex Extension**: Elaborate substantively on the path toward non-convex analysis beyond Remark 2. Discuss specific technical barriers and potential approaches.

3. **Practical Implications**: Add discussion connecting the theoretical bounds to practical algorithm design. For instance, how do the bounds suggest tuning the number of inner-loop steps or learning rates?

4. **Manuscript Quality**: Carefully proofread and correct the typos and formatting issues identified above.

## Minor Comments

- The tightness of the O(1/mn) bound in the large K regime deserves more discussion, particularly regarding whether this limitation reflects fundamental hardness or room for improvement.
- The assumption of non-atomic distributions is reasonable but could be discussed more explicitly in the main text rather than relegated to remarks.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid theoretical contribution to understanding MAML generalization through a new stability framework and improved bounds. The introduction of (γ, K)-uniform stability is well-motivated, and the analysis of both recurring and unseen tasks is comprehensive. However, the restriction to strongly convex losses and single-step adaptation significantly limits the practical relevance of the results. The gap between these theoretical assumptions and the non-convex, multi-step setting used in practice remains substantial and inadequately addressed.

Despite these limitations, the theoretical insights are valuable for the meta-learning community, and the paper represents a meaningful advance in our understanding of MAML's generalization properties. The work would benefit from more thorough discussion of its practical implications and clearer articulation of the barriers to extending the analysis to more realistic settings.

**Recommendation**: The paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold. It makes sufficient theoretical contributions to warrant publication, but the restrictive assumptions and limited practical applicability prevent it from being a strong accept. The authors should address the technical corrections and consider expanding the discussion of limitations and potential extensions.

Summary:
The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.

Main Review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.

reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.

Main Review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Generalization Bounds for MAML Algorithms

## Summary

This paper establishes generalization bounds for Meta-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in two complementary settings: recurring tasks and unseen tasks. For recurring tasks under strong convexity, the authors achieve an O(1/mn) convergence rate depending on both the number of tasks (m) and samples per task (n). For unseen tasks, bounds are derived in terms of total variation distance between task distributions. The theoretical contributions are sound and novel, though the work is limited by its restrictive convexity assumptions and lack of analysis for the bilevel optimization formulation standard in practice.

## Strengths

1. **Improved Convergence Rates**: The paper provides generalization bounds that explicitly depend on both m and n, achieving O(1/mn) for recurring tasks. This represents a meaningful improvement over prior work (e.g., [28]) which achieved only O(1/m) rates, demonstrating tighter analysis.

2. **Comprehensive Scenario Coverage**: By analyzing both recurring and unseen task settings, the work provides a more complete theoretical picture of MAML generalization. The distinction between these scenarios is practically motivated and theoretically interesting.

3. **Novel Stability Framework**: The introduction of (γ, K)-uniformly stable definition is a genuine contribution that captures the role of data points per task. This refined stability notion, which modifies K+1 data points rather than the entire dataset, enables tighter bounds and represents an improvement over previous stability definitions in the meta-learning context.

4. **Practical Motivation**: The analysis addresses a realistic concern—that training typically involves multiple passes over fixed data rather than fresh samples at each iteration—which enhances the practical relevance of the theoretical results.

5. **Sound Technical Approach**: The coupling technique used to eliminate K-dependence in unseen task bounds and the formal connection between algorithmic stability and generalization (Theorem 1) are technically competent.

## Weaknesses

1. **Restrictive Convexity Assumption**: The limitation to strongly convex losses is a significant constraint that severely limits applicability to modern deep learning. While the authors acknowledge this limitation, the paper provides no insight into whether or how the analysis might extend to non-convex settings, which are essential for practical MAML applications.

2. **Lack of Clarity on Non-Convex Barriers**: The paper does not adequately explain why the proposed (γ, K)-uniformly stable definition prevents extension to non-convex problems. This is a critical gap—readers are left uncertain about whether the limitation is fundamental to the approach or merely reflects the current analysis technique.

3. **Missing Bilevel Optimization Analysis**: MAML is fundamentally formulated as a bilevel optimization problem, yet this paper analyzes only the single-level case. This disconnect between the theoretical framework and how MAML is actually implemented in practice is a notable limitation. The paper should at minimum discuss how results would adapt to bilevel formulations or explain why such analysis is intractable.

4. **Tightness Concerns**: While the bounds are tight in the small K regime (typical for few-shot learning), they are not tight for large K, limiting the generality of the results.

5. **Absence of Experimental Validation**: Despite being a theory paper, the work includes no experimental verification of the theoretical bounds. Even simple empirical validation demonstrating that the predicted rates hold in practice would strengthen confidence in the results.

6. **Incomplete Discussion of Limitations**: The paper lacks a dedicated discussion of its limitations and does not address potential societal impacts, which are increasingly expected in academic publications.

## Technical Comments

- The improvement from O(1/m) to O(1/mn) bounds is achieved through careful application of the stability framework, which is well-executed.
- The analysis cleanly separates training error and generalization error before combining them for total excess loss.
- The technical development is sound, though the restriction to strong convexity limits its broader relevance.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Clarify Non-Convex Limitations**: Provide a more detailed explanation—ideally with concrete examples—of why (γ, K)-uniform stability cannot be extended to non-convex settings. If relevant discussions occurred during author response, incorporate this material into the paper itself.

2. **Address Bilevel Optimization**: Discuss how the results might adapt to the standard bilevel MAML formulation, or explain the fundamental challenges in applying stability analysis to bilevel programming. Consider referencing existing work on bilevel stability.

3. **Empirical Validation**: Include at least some experimental verification of the theoretical predictions, such as plots showing how generalization error scales with m and n in a simple convex setting.

4. **Expand Limitations Section**: Add explicit discussion of the work's limitations, including the convexity requirement and the gap between the analyzed single-level formulation and practical bilevel MAML implementations.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes solid theoretical contributions to understanding MAML generalization through a novel stability-based analysis. The improved O(1/mn) bounds and the new stability framework represent genuine advances. However, the restriction to strongly convex settings, the lack of analysis for the bilevel formulation central to MAML, and the absence of experimental validation limit the impact. The paper would benefit from clearer exposition of its fundamental limitations and more explicit connections to practical MAML implementations.

Despite these limitations, the theoretical results are new, technically sound, and represent a meaningful contribution to the meta-learning theory literature. The work is marginally above the acceptance threshold and would be suitable for publication with the understanding that it addresses a restricted setting of theoretical interest rather than providing immediately applicable insights for practical deep learning.

**Recommendation: Accept (marginally above threshold)**
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.

Main Review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

Main Review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Generalization and Stability Analysis of MAML

## Summary

This paper presents a theoretical analysis of the Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithm, providing generalization error and uniform stability bounds. The authors establish an O(1/mn) generalization bound for strongly convex objectives when the test task belongs to the training task distribution, and derive bounds involving total variation distance for unseen tasks. The paper is well-written and addresses an important problem in meta-learning theory, though the novelty of the results and empirical validation raise some concerns.

## Strengths

The paper is clearly written with well-defined notations, assumptions, and explanations that make it accessible to readers. The motivation for studying generalization in MAML-type algorithms is compelling, as this remains an important open problem in meta-learning.

A key contribution is the proposed (γ, K)-uniform stability definition (Definition 3), which elegantly captures the dependence on both the number of tasks (m) and the number of samples per task (n). This represents an improvement over prior work such as Chen et al. (2020) by providing a more nuanced stability characterization appropriate for the meta-learning setting.

The paper demonstrates that the number of intra-task training samples (n) plays an important role in reducing generalization error, which provides useful insights into the meta-learning dynamics. Additionally, the authors establish optimal dependence on the total number of training samples (mn) for strongly convex objectives, and employ a coupling technique to eliminate dependence on K in the total variation distance characterization—a notable technical contribution.

## Weaknesses

Despite these contributions, several significant concerns limit the impact of this work. Most fundamentally, the main generalization bound showing O(1/mn) dependence is not particularly surprising. In standard supervised learning, generalization error naturally decreases with the total number of training samples, so observing this behavior in the meta-learning setting is somewhat expected rather than revealing new insights specific to MAML.

The paper does not adequately capture the special structure of MAML or the interplay between its training and test procedures. The analysis appears to largely apply standard statistical learning theory rather than demonstrating insights unique to the composite meta-learning framework. The training error characterization primarily builds on existing convergence results (Fallah et al., 2020), and the stability analysis relies on standard techniques from Bousquet & Elisseeff (2002). It remains unclear what specifically challenging technical obstacles arise when extending these existing techniques to the MAML setting.

Furthermore, the paper does not provide evidence that the derived bounds are tight beyond the small K regime, nor does it demonstrate optimality for other parameters such as the test sample size K. The lack of new insights compared to Chen et al. (2020) regarding how the support/query split affects generalization is also a missed opportunity for deeper understanding.

The restriction to strongly convex loss functions is a significant limitation, as this assumption does not hold for practical neural network applications where MAML is typically employed. This substantially reduces the practical relevance of the theoretical guarantees.

## Empirical Validation

The empirical evaluation is insufficient and does not adequately validate the theoretical findings. The experiments show large variations in the curves and lack statistical rigor—results are presented without averaging over sufficient random seeds. Critically, the experiments only verify the impact of m and n on generalization, leaving key predictions unvalidated. The paper does not provide experiments examining the dependence on test sample size K, nor does it verify whether the total variation distance metric actually captures generalization error for unseen tasks. The experimental scope is limited to toy problems, with no evaluation on supervised classification—an important application domain for meta-learning.

## Technical Comments

The analysis assumes strongly convex loss functions and requires the constraint α ≤ min{1/(2L), μ/(8ρG)} to preserve strong convexity. The generalization bound includes the term O(G²(1+αLK)/(mnμ)), showing complex dependence on multiple problem parameters. While the coupling technique from Hollander (2012) is interesting, the overall technical approach does not appear to involve novel methodological innovations beyond adapting existing frameworks.

## Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen this work, I recommend the following:

1. **Empirical rigor**: Conduct experiments with multiple random seeds and report averaged results with confidence intervals to establish statistical significance.

2. **Validate theoretical predictions**: Design experiments to verify the impact of test sample size K on generalization and test whether the total variation distance metric meaningfully captures generalization error for unseen tasks.

3. **Expand experimental scope**: Include experiments on supervised classification tasks and demonstrate results beyond toy problems.

4. **Clarify technical contributions**: Explicitly articulate what technical challenges arise specifically in the MAML setting and how they differ from standard analyses.

5. **Provide actionable insights**: If the total variation distance characterization is validated empirically, translate these findings into practical guidelines for task selection in meta-learning.

6. **Establish tightness**: Provide evidence that the bounds are tight in relevant regimes or establish matching lower bounds.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid contribution to meta-learning theory by providing generalization bounds and introducing a stability definition suited to the meta-learning setting. However, the results largely confirm expected behavior from standard learning theory rather than revealing insights specific to MAML's structure. The empirical validation is insufficient, and the restriction to strongly convex objectives limits practical applicability. While the work is technically sound and well-presented, it falls marginally above the acceptance threshold due to its contributions to meta-learning theory, though significant improvements in empirical validation and deeper technical insights would substantially strengthen the work.

**Recommendation: Accept (marginally above threshold)**

reviews.main_review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Generalization Analysis of MAML via Stability

## Summary

This paper contributes to the theoretical understanding of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) by introducing a new stability-based definition for meta-learning algorithms that accounts for both the number of tasks (m) and samples per task (n). The authors provide generalization error characterization for both recurring and unseen tasks, along with a separate treatment of training and generalization error components. While the theoretical contribution is meaningful, significant concerns about the practical relevance and tightness of the derived bounds limit the impact of this work.

## Strengths

1. **Novel Stability Framework**: The paper introduces a well-motivated stability definition for meta-learning that explicitly captures the dependence on both m and n, which is a natural and useful extension of classical stability concepts to the meta-learning setting.

2. **Comprehensive Analysis**: The work provides generalization characterization for both recurring and unseen tasks, offering a more complete picture of MAML's behavior than some prior analyses.

3. **Systematic Treatment**: The separation of training error and generalization error in the analysis provides useful insights into different sources of error in the meta-learning framework.

## Major Weaknesses

The most critical issue with this work is the **counterintuitive dependence of the generalization bound on K (the number of shots per task)**. The bound exhibits O(K/mn) dependence, which implies that generalization error *increases* with more samples per task—a result that directly contradicts extensive empirical evidence in the MAML literature and undermines the practical utility of the theoretical results. This is not merely a loose bound; it suggests a fundamental mismatch between the theory and practice of MAML.

Related to this concern, the bound becomes as loose as standard SGD analysis when K is small, which raises the question of whether the meta-learning framework provides any theoretical advantage in the practically relevant regime. Only in the large K regime (discussed in Appendix E) does the error decrease with K, but this requires impractically large K values (the authors note ~10,000 shots for 1M total samples), making this regime largely irrelevant for practical applications.

### Technical Concerns

The strong convexity and smoothness assumptions are extremely restrictive and do not hold for neural networks, which are the primary application domain for MAML. While the authors acknowledge this limitation, the gap between these assumptions and practical neural network settings is substantial enough to question the applicability of the results.

Additionally, there is a technical question regarding the stability definition around equation 569: it is unclear whether the loss of task i is affected only by replacement of K data points in S_i^n, or also by replacements in S_j^n (j ≠ i). This ambiguity affects the validity of the subsequent analysis, particularly the claim that expectations of w_S and w_̃S are equivalent.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Lack of Empirical Validation**: The theoretical results, particularly Theorem 2 regarding generalization error on seen tasks, lack any empirical verification. Simple experiments validating the K-dependence behavior would substantially strengthen the paper.

2. **Presentation Issues**: The discussion in Appendix E, which attempts to address the K-dependence problem, should be integrated into the main paper if it provides meaningful insights. Currently, relegating this to the appendix obscures an important limitation.

3. **Limited Discussion of Assumptions**: While the restrictiveness of the strong convexity and smoothness assumptions is mentioned, the paper would benefit from more thorough discussion of how these assumptions affect the practical relevance of the results.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Conduct empirical experiments to validate or refute the predicted relationship between K and generalization error. This is essential for assessing whether the bound captures meaningful behavior.

2. Provide simple experiments validating Theorem 2, even in restricted settings where the theoretical assumptions approximately hold.

3. Clarify the technical details regarding the stability definition and the treatment of dependencies between tasks.

4. If Appendix E provides meaningful analysis of the K-dependence issue, incorporate this discussion into the main narrative with clearer exposition of when and why the large K regime might be relevant.

5. Expand the discussion of how the strong convexity assumptions limit applicability and whether similar analysis could be extended to more general settings.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a theoretical contribution to meta-learning analysis through a new stability-based framework. However, the practical utility of the derived bounds is questionable due to the problematic K-dependence and restrictive assumptions. The fact that the bounds do not improve over standard SGD analysis in the practically relevant small-K regime, combined with the counterintuitive increase in error with more samples, suggests that either the bounds are too loose or the analysis misses important aspects of why MAML works in practice. Empirical validation and clarification of the technical details would be necessary to strengthen the contribution.

The paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold due to its novel theoretical framework and systematic treatment of the problem, but the concerns raised above prevent a stronger recommendation. The work will be of interest primarily to the theory community, with limited immediate impact on understanding or improving practical MAML algorithms.

Summary:
The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.

Main Review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The assumption of strong convexity and smoothness is very strong and does not hold for most neural networks. The authors have not fully addressed such limitation.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The authors present generalization bounds for MAML when test tasks are the same as the ones seen during training (recurring tasks) or when they are different but sampled from the same distribution (unseen tasks). The main takeaways are a. for recurring tasks, the excess population loss is bounded by O(1/mn) with m the number of tasks and n the number of data points, and b. generalization to unseen tasks depends on the distance (total variation) between train and test task distributions.
reviews.main_review:
The results of the paper are novel and of interest to the multi-task/meta-learning community. In particular, the generalization result for recurring tasks (Th. 1 & 2) refines multi-tasks bounds for MAML when data is limited. This is important since limited data is often the main motivation for these types of meta-learning methods. While the approach to get those bounds builds on the stability of SGD, the proposed stability definition is tailored to MAML and lends itself to further study and extensions. The second result (Th. 3) directly targets transfer to new tasks, formalizing one of the main intuitions behind MAML: the closer the train and test tasks, the most effective the transfer. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work directly assessing generalization for MAML when train and test task sets are disjoint.

It helps that the paper is clearly written and introduces all necessary background to MAML. For each theorem, assumptions are clearly stated and the results are discussed from both a theoretical and practical perspective. Although a bit dense, I enjoyed reading this paper.

The main limitation of the paper is convexity of the meta-learning loss — but similar publications also considered this setting. Specifically, I would like to see the assumptions on the fast-adaptation learning rate (alpha) relaxed, since they are rarely met in practice.

Finally, I wished the related work section were a bit more expansive. For example, issues of generalization were also considered in [1], and [2] discussed issues related to the number of samples per tasks. How does the present work differ?

References:

[1] "Global Convergence and Generalization Bound of Gradient-Based Meta-Learning with Deep Neural Nets", Wang et al., ArXiv 2020.

[2] "How to distribute data across tasks for meta-learning?", Cioba et al., ArXiv 2021.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations are clearly stated, societal impact is not mentioned.
