reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new High-dim BO framework inspired by Block coordinate ascent. Although similar, this paper differs from other works which leverages a low dimensional structure assumption in that it utilizes all the dimension in the function space. Aside from the overall scheme, its other contributions include (1) an update scheme for the coordinate sampling distribution; (2) a heuristic stopping rule to balance between exploiting current coordinate block and exploring new coordinate blocks; and (3) a theoretical analysis that upper bounds the total regret on a surrogate loss function. The framework shows some promising results when compared with other high-dim BO works.
reviews.main_review:
--Clarity
The paper is generally well written and easy to follow. I have some minor confusions:

1. In the algorithm, the posterior is described as p(\hat{f}| \hat{H}). Is the posterior specified by a GP with low dimensional kernel or high dimensional kernel, but the length-scales on inactive coordinates are frozen (not learned)?.

2. At the beginning of section 4.2, it was claimed that CobBO was compared to REMBO and ATPE but I don't see the comparison anywhere. Also, what would  BOHB be in Fig.5 ?

--Originality
The motivation behind doing BO along a subset of coordinates has been explored many times in the literature. This paper argues that the proposed method, CobBO, "efficiently leverages all the observations in the whole space ... rather than only relying on limited observations in each coordinate subspace". As far as I understand, DROPOUT [35] also re-selects the active coordinates at every iteration (and fill in the remaining coordinates using some heuristic strategy), which guarantees making use of all coordinates in the long run. However, I believe that the proposed subspace selection algorithm is more informed due to the update scheme of the sampling distribution.
--Quality
My main concern with this paper is that the theoretical analysis is not conventional (with respect to the BO literature). In my experience, BO regret analysis usually measures the regret on the objective function itself rather than a surrogate loss. It could be useful to assert that the sampling scheme will, in the asymptotic sense, always pick the right subspace to improve the objective the most number of times; but it does not inform us by how much does it improve the objective. It seems to me that the proposed regret bound is an analysis of the coordinate descent aspect alone, and is entirely independent of the optimization routine within each selected subspace (i.e., if I replace BO by something else, would anything change?). 

Please refer to the Significance subsection for my comments on the experiments.

--Significance
1. For some experiments (e.g., Levy 200D and Ackley 200D), I don't think all methods have converged by the end of 10000 iterations. It would probably take longer to conclude that one is clearly superior to another. 
2. I would suggest some ablation study to show the effect of having variable coordinate block size |C_t| (as I understand, it is now capped at 30). On average, how many coordinates are sampled per iterations? Is there a lower bound to prevent the scheme from selecting none? How does it affect performance if we increase/decrease the upper bound?
3. It was claimed that REMBO takes too long for Ackley 200D, but I dont see how it is the case if we limit the number of effective dimension (to be roughly the same as the average no. selected coordinates by CobBO)? Seems to me that both REMBO and CobBO optimize a low dimensional GP at every iteration with the same no. observations, so what makes one significantly slower than the other?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors have described the Broader impact of this paper, which I find reasonable. I believe there would be no potential negative societal impact.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new High-dim BO framework inspired by Block coordinate ascent. Although similar, this paper differs from other works which leverages a low dimensional structure assumption in that it utilizes all the dimension in the function space. Aside from the overall scheme, its other contributions include (1) an update scheme for the coordinate sampling distribution; (2) a heuristic stopping rule to balance between exploiting current coordinate block and exploring new coordinate blocks; and (3) a theoretical analysis that upper bounds the total regret on a surrogate loss function. The framework shows some promising results when compared with other high-dim BO works.

Main Review:
--Clarity
The paper is generally well written and easy to follow. I have some minor confusions:

1. In the algorithm, the posterior is described as p(\hat{f}| \hat{H}). Is the posterior specified by a GP with low dimensional kernel or high dimensional kernel, but the length-scales on inactive coordinates are frozen (not learned)?.

2. At the beginning of section 4.2, it was claimed that CobBO was compared to REMBO and ATPE but I don't see the comparison anywhere. Also, what would  BOHB be in Fig.5 ?

--Originality
The motivation behind doing BO along a subset of coordinates has been explored many times in the literature. This paper argues that the proposed method, CobBO, "efficiently leverages all the observations in the whole space ... rather than only relying on limited observations in each coordinate subspace". As far as I understand, DROPOUT [35] also re-selects the active coordinates at every iteration (and fill in the remaining coordinates using some heuristic strategy), which guarantees making use of all coordinates in the long run. However, I believe that the proposed subspace selection algorithm is more informed due to the update scheme of the sampling distribution.
--Quality
My main concern with this paper is that the theoretical analysis is not conventional (with respect to the BO literature). In my experience, BO regret analysis usually measures the regret on the objective function itself rather than a surrogate loss. It could be useful to assert that the sampling scheme will, in the asymptotic sense, always pick the right subspace to improve the objective the most number of times; but it does not inform us by how much does it improve the objective. It seems to me that the proposed regret bound is an analysis of the coordinate descent aspect alone, and is entirely independent of the optimization routine within each selected subspace (i.e., if I replace BO by something else, would anything change?). 

Please refer to the Significance subsection for my comments on the experiments.

--Significance
1. For some experiments (e.g., Levy 200D and Ackley 200D), I don't think all methods have converged by the end of 10000 iterations. It would probably take longer to conclude that one is clearly superior to another. 
2. I would suggest some ablation study to show the effect of having variable coordinate block size |C_t| (as I understand, it is now capped at 30). On average, how many coordinates are sampled per iterations? Is there a lower bound to prevent the scheme from selecting none? How does it affect performance if we increase/decrease the upper bound?
3. It was claimed that REMBO takes too long for Ackley 200D, but I dont see how it is the case if we limit the number of effective dimension (to be roughly the same as the average no. selected coordinates by CobBO)? Seems to me that both REMBO and CobBO optimize a low dimensional GP at every iteration with the same no. observations, so what makes one significantly slower than the other?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have described the Broader impact of this paper, which I find reasonable. I believe there would be no potential negative societal impact.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: CobBO - High-Dimensional Bayesian Optimization via Block Coordinate Ascent

## Summary

This paper introduces CobBO, a Bayesian optimization framework for high-dimensional problems that employs block coordinate ascent with a two-stage kernel approach. The work tackles an important problem and presents a generally well-motivated solution. However, significant concerns regarding the theoretical analysis, experimental validation, and clarity of presentation prevent me from recommending acceptance at this time.

## Strengths

The paper is well-written and clearly motivated, making the core ideas accessible. The authors present a novel perspective on leveraging observations across the entire high-dimensional space rather than limiting themselves to observations within each selected coordinate subspace. This is a genuine conceptual contribution that distinguishes the work from purely local coordinate selection approaches.

The proposed sampling distribution update scheme for subspace selection is more principled than prior work, offering a more informed approach to coordinate selection at each iteration. The empirical results are promising when benchmarked against other high-dimensional BO methods, and the motivation for applying block coordinate ascent to the BO setting is clearly articulated.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Non-Standard Theoretical Analysis

The theoretical contribution deviates significantly from standard BO regret analysis in a problematic way. Rather than bounding regret with respect to the objective function itself, the analysis bounds regret on a surrogate loss function. This is unconventional and raises questions about what the bounds actually guarantee in practice. More critically, the regret bound appears to analyze only the coordinate descent aspect in isolation, independent of the BO routine executed within each selected subspace. The connection between minimizing surrogate loss in coordinate selection and actual improvement on the objective function remains unclear and inadequately justified.

**Suggestion**: Provide a theoretical analysis that directly bounds regret on the objective function. Clarify how the coordinate selection regret bound translates to guarantees about the actual optimization performance within the BO framework.

### 2. Incomplete and Unclear Experimental Comparisons

The paper claims comparisons with REMBO and ATPE, yet these methods do not appear in Figure 5. Additionally, BOHB is mentioned in the text but its role and relevance to Figure 5 are unclear. These omissions make it difficult to assess the empirical claims and suggest the experimental section was not finalized properly.

**Suggestion**: Complete all experimental comparisons mentioned in the text and include all baseline methods in the result figures. Clarify which methods are compared in which experiments.

### 3. Questionable Convergence on High-Dimensional Problems

For challenging high-dimensional problems (Levy 200D and Ackley 200D), 10,000 iterations may be insufficient for methods to converge. Claims of superiority on these problems are therefore premature and potentially misleading. Running experiments to true convergence or substantially higher iteration counts would be necessary to substantiate these claims.

**Suggestion**: Extend experiments on high-dimensional problems to more iterations to ensure convergence and establish clear performance differences.

### 4. Missing Ablation Studies

The paper lacks ablation studies on critical design choices. Notably, the coordinate block size |C_t| is capped at 30 without justification or empirical analysis. There is no investigation of how this parameter affects performance, nor is there information about the average number of coordinates sampled per iteration or any lower bounds preventing degenerate cases.

**Suggestion**: Provide ablation studies examining the effect of variable coordinate block size, the distribution of coordinates sampled per iteration, and any lower bounds on coordinate selection.

## Technical Issues

**Posterior Distribution Specification**: Algorithm 1 is ambiguous about the posterior specification. It is unclear whether the method uses a low-dimensional kernel applied to the selected subspace, or a high-dimensional kernel with frozen length-scales on inactive coordinates. This distinction has important implications for the method's behavior and must be clarified.

**Computational Efficiency Claims**: The computational advantage over REMBO is not convincingly demonstrated. If both methods optimize low-dimensional GPs with similar numbers of observations at each iteration, the source of CobBO's claimed significant speedup is unclear. A detailed computational cost comparison would strengthen this claim.

**Similarity to Prior Work**: The approach shares conceptual similarities with prior work (e.g., DROPOUT) that also re-selects coordinates at each iteration. While the sampling distribution update scheme may be more informed, the novelty and practical advantages over such methods deserve more thorough discussion.

## Minor Issues

- Missing details on how inactive coordinates are handled in the posterior
- No explanation for why REMBO takes substantially longer than CobBO given similar low-dimensional GP optimization
- Insufficient discussion of the relationship between the proposed method and related coordinate selection approaches

## Recommendation

While this paper addresses an important problem and presents promising empirical results, the non-standard theoretical analysis, incomplete experimental comparisons, and missing ablation studies are significant concerns. The theoretical contributions do not provide conventional BO guarantees, and the experimental validation is not sufficiently thorough to compensate for this weakness. The work would benefit from substantial revisions addressing the theoretical analysis, completing all experimental comparisons, and providing comprehensive ablation studies before it meets the acceptance threshold.

**Decision: Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold**

The paper shows promise but requires major revisions to address the theoretical concerns and experimental gaps before it is suitable for publication.

title: Distributional Generalization: Characterizing Classifiers Beyond Test Error

reviews.summary: The paper empirically characterizes a fine-grained generalization property of interpolating learning algorithms, and give a conjecture that formalizes the observed phenomenon. Roughly, the paper observes that the generalization properties of an interpolating classifier can be quite distinct for different sample subpopulations with the same label, whenever the label noise imposed on these subpopulations during the training phase is distinct. Authors conjecture that the generalization properties may be uniform only for samples belonging to a smaller "distinguishable" clusters of the input data space.
reviews.main_review:
Post-rebuttal: I thank authors for their detailed response. Most of the authors' response made a perfect sense. One thing that prevented me from raising the score further is that I am still not sure about is whether the conjecture itself is meaningful or useful. In fact, after reading the response to my first point (Converse...), I am slightly more pessimistic about the possibility that "distinguishability" and "feature calibration" are tightly connected concepts (say, in a causal sense). I am curious if there are alternate (and perhaps more useful) conjectures that can help explain the phenomenon. In other words, I think the paper should benefit from having an extensive discussion that justifies why the proposed conjecture based on distinguishability is nontrivial, useful, and better than other possible alternatives.


---

__What I like.__ The reported empirical observation is intriguing to some degree, and (in my opinion) is worthy of documenting and formalizing. Authors validate the observation under a variety of scenarios in Section 1 & 4 (and also appendices), which helped convincing me of the existence of such phenomenon. As the authors noted, establishing a theoretical framework that can explain the depicted phenomenon may be an interesting open problem (perhaps we can phrase problems in a minimax style with multiple tasks?). The clarity of the writing is well above average, while there are several points that can be clarified further. I also liked the theoretical contributions, which could help readers grasp the idea of how such conjectures can be proved.

__Checking/refining the conjecture.__ On the one hand, I find that the provided empirical observations support the conjecture well. On the other hand, I think that one can even further strengthen the argument by trying to provide an evidence supporting that the conditions described in the conjecture is indeed essential for the distributional similarity. I am actually very curious whether the "distinguishable features" are really essential. Is there a case where the distributional similarity does not hold for indistinguishable features, which is perhaps more informative than the perfectly random case? Although this is not really necessary if one is interested only in the validity of the conjecture, having such evidence will help us understand how non-vacuous/"tight" the conjecture is.

__Clarifying the terminology: Interpolating.__ I think the term "interpolating classifier" can be clarified further in the text. Although the terminology is now (sort of) popularly used in the literature, I think still they need to be properly defined at least once in the main text, as the modern use of this terminology are often wider than what is used to mean in the classic kernel-based literature.

__Clarifying the terminology: Distributional Closeness.__ I am curious in what sense the notion of "distributional closeness" introduced in Section 2 is different from the widely used notion of integral probability metrics (IPMs). If they are identical, sticking to the existing notation would help reduce the confusion.

__Practical implication.__ While I mostly liked the theoretical implications illustrated in Section 1.3, I wonder if authors could give an idea why capturing such fine-grained generalization properties may be important. For instance, could there be any implications to the fairness or shortcut learning?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitation is clearly described, in my opinion. This work mainly discusses what is implicitly taking place in the standard learning practices, instead of giving a new one. I do not think there is any "potential negative impact" that should be discussed.

reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

__Practical implication.__ While I mostly liked the theoretical implications illustrated in Section 1.3, I wonder if authors could give an idea why capturing such fine-grained generalization properties may be important. For instance, could there be any implications to the fairness or shortcut learning?

The limitation is clearly described, in my opinion. This work mainly discusses what is implicitly taking place in the standard learning practices, instead of giving a new one. I do not think there is any "potential negative impact" that should be discussed.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Feature Calibration in Interpolating Classifiers

## Summary

This paper documents and formalizes an intriguing empirical phenomenon: interpolating classifiers exhibit distributional similarity between training and test sets when features are "distinguishable." While the empirical observations are compelling and well-documented, I have concerns about whether the proposed Feature Calibration conjecture represents the most meaningful and useful explanation for the observed behavior, or whether alternative formulations might be equally or more appropriate.

## Strengths

The paper makes several noteworthy contributions. First, the core empirical observation is genuinely interesting and warrants formal investigation. The phenomenon is thoroughly validated across diverse scenarios—including multiple model architectures (neural networks, kernel machines, decision trees) and experimental settings documented in the main text and appendices. This breadth of validation substantially strengthens confidence in the empirical claims.

The writing quality is commendable, presenting complex ideas with clarity and accessibility. The theoretical contribution—a proof of Feature Calibration for 1-Nearest-Neighbor classifiers under mild regularity assumptions—provides valuable intuition about how such conjectures might be formally established, serving an important pedagogical function. Finally, the paper frames an interesting open problem that could be naturally formulated in minimax terms across multiple tasks, potentially inspiring future work.

## Major Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, several significant concerns limit the contribution:

**1. Unclear Practical and Theoretical Significance**

The paper documents an empirical phenomenon but does not adequately justify why the proposed conjecture formulation is meaningful or useful. The connection between distinguishable features and distributional similarity remains somewhat opaque. More critically, the paper lacks discussion of practical implications—how might practitioners use these insights? Are there connections to important problems such as fairness, shortcut learning, or other domains where understanding feature-level generalization matters?

**2. Insufficient Justification for the Conjecture Formulation**

The paper does not convincingly argue that distinguishability is the right lens through which to understand this phenomenon. The causal relationship between distinguishable features and distributional similarity is asserted rather than rigorously established. Notably, the paper does not provide evidence that distributional similarity *fails* for indistinguishable features (beyond trivial cases like perfectly random features), which would be necessary to demonstrate the conjecture's tightness and essentiality. Without such evidence, alternative characterizations may be equally valid.

**3. Limited Exploration of Alternative Explanations**

The paper does not adequately explore or discuss other conjectures that might explain the same empirical observations. Are there simpler or more interpretable characterizations? Could the phenomenon be explained through existing theoretical frameworks? This narrow focus on a single formulation weakens the contribution's impact.

**4. Technical Clarity Issues**

Several technical points require clarification:
- The term "interpolating classifier" is central to the work but lacks a formal definition in the main text. Modern usage of this term extends beyond classical kernel-based literature, and this should be explicitly addressed.
- The distributional closeness notion introduced in Section 2 appears potentially equivalent to integral probability metrics (IPMs), yet this relationship is never discussed. If equivalent, the paper should use established notation; if different, the distinction should be clarified.

## Minor Weaknesses

The definition of distinguishable features (Definition 1) depends on multiple factors—training procedure, data distribution, sample size, and approximation parameter ε—yet the paper does not thoroughly discuss how sensitive the conjecture is to these dependencies or how one should choose these hyperparameters in practice.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Strengthen Justification**: Provide extensive discussion explaining why the Feature Calibration conjecture based on distinguishability is nontrivial and superior to alternative formulations. What makes this particular characterization the "right" one?

2. **Demonstrate Necessity**: Test whether distributional similarity fails for indistinguishable features in non-trivial settings. This would provide evidence that distinguishability is truly essential, not merely sufficient.

3. **Clarify Technical Definitions**: Formally define "interpolating classifier" in the main text with appropriate breadth. Explicitly address the relationship between your distributional closeness notion and existing IPM frameworks.

4. **Discuss Alternatives**: Explore and discuss competing conjectures that might explain the observed phenomenon, explaining why Feature Calibration is preferable.

5. **Articulate Practical Implications**: Connect these theoretical insights to real-world applications or downstream problems where fine-grained understanding of feature-level generalization provides value.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a well-executed empirical study documenting an interesting phenomenon in interpolating classifiers. The experimental validation is thorough, the writing is clear, and the theoretical contribution for 1-NN provides useful intuition. However, the work falls short of making a fully compelling case that the proposed Feature Calibration conjecture is the most meaningful formulation of this phenomenon. The lack of evidence that distinguishability is truly essential, combined with limited exploration of alternatives and unclear practical implications, prevents this from being a strong contribution.

The paper makes a solid incremental contribution to our understanding of interpolating classifiers and merits publication as a well-documented empirical finding with an interesting open problem. However, addressing the concerns above—particularly strengthening justification for the conjecture and demonstrating its necessity—would substantially improve the work.

**Recommendation**: Accept (marginally above threshold)

The paper's thorough empirical validation, clarity of presentation, and interesting open problem justify acceptance, but significant revisions addressing the conjecture's formulation and practical significance are recommended.

reviews.summary:
This submission proposes a new type of generalization satisfied by interpolating classifiers (the primary focus), that is different from agreement between test error and train error as in classical learning theory. This type of generalization is largely motivated by empirical evidence across different models (neural networks, kernel regression & svm, decision trees) and theoretically for 1-Nearest Neighbors classification (Theorem 1). More specifically, the submission claims that for certain types of mappings x -> L(x) (termed "distinguishable features" -- i.e. they must be learnable to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration), the joint distribution of (L(x), y) where (x,y) pairs are drawn from the true distribution will be "close" to the joint distribution of (L(x), f(x)) on the test set, where f(x) is the trained model prediction.

List of contributions:
--Proposes that interpolating classifiers satisfy "distributional generalization." This is first more specifically formulated as the "Feature Calibration Conjecture" (FCC) in Section 3. A crucial component of this is to identify the types of features L(x) for which the calibration holds (Definition 1); roughly, if the feature is learnable from inputs x to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration (fixed algorithm, data distribution, number of samples, allowable error), then under that same setting calibration will be achieved. The authors prove FCC is true for 1-Nearest neighbors. The Feature Calibration Conjecture is not fully specified, in that there is an assumption of "natural distribution" D (which is such that the conjecture holds in the empirical settings considered).

--Empirical test of the FCC. The types of distinguishable features L considered include (i) the constant partition (calibration then implies the marginal distribution p(y) of the predicted labels matches that of the true label distribution); (ii) a "coarse partition" (L(x) is an "easy" classification task) and calibration holds for fine-grained label subgroups, for instance in Imagenet; (iii) "class partition" (classes that can be learned well from standard datasets, and the true label distribution is a noisy corruption of this); (iv) multiple features. Models investigated include neural networks (WideResnets and MLPs), kernels (standard and high-performing ones), and decision trees. 

--A definition for "distributional generalization" which subsumes the definition of feature calibration & classical generalization, and very preliminary evidence that this holds for non-interpolating models (a Wide Resnet model on CIFAR-10 during training).
reviews.main_review:
Originality: This work identifies & investigates an original empirical property that appears to be satisfied by interpolating classifiers. The proposed concepts (Feature Calibration) are general & seem distinct from any prior work to the best of my knowledge. (For example, the authors note that "standard" calibration is a particular case of Feature Calibration with specific choice of L(x).) I think this work is bold in what it seeks to accomplish (asking a fundamentally new question at the basis of learning theory, building evidence from diverse empirical examples) and makes a reasonable first step towards doing so. 

Quality: The quality of the work seems high. The authors give a number of different empirical examples with sufficient coverage across model diversity and provide a theoretically satisfied case (1-Nearest Neighbors). The authors note the limitations of the work (e.g. the FCC is specified in terms of "natural distributions" which has an unclear meaning) and are fairly thorough in addressing potential concerns & connections to other works. I was not able to identify significant shortcomings with respect to the conceptual formulation or message of the paper. I make some comments about diversity of empirical examples (with respect to datasets / partitions) below.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and was straightforward to read.

Significance: I think this paper takes a risk in its premise, provides a reasonable amount of evidence in support of its main claims, and would spur further work in this area; as I was also not able to identify any major issues with the paper, this is the reason behind my suggested score. In Section 1.3 the authors discuss a number of consequences for learning theory which seem notable. This work tells us more about the implicit bias of models (though not just neural networks); most interesting I think is that in certain limits (e.g. when models are interpolating, and model size >> dataset size), models are not approaching the Bayes optimal classifier but sample from the distribution p(y|x). 

Other comments & questions:

--I find some mentions of "Bayes optimal classifier" to be confusing at times -- for instance, its usage in L268 seems to be with respect to argmax p(y | L(x)) (rather than argmax p(y|x)), if I understand correctly. Perhaps it is worth pointing this out and the connection between the two (e.g. in the setting where p(y|x) is discussed, section starting at L107). 

--In the empirical examples with label noise, why do the authors consider only sparse confusion matrices?

--I think this work has good empirical diversity across very distinct classes of interpolating models, but one place where the paper could be noticeably strengthened is diversity of examples with respect to the datasets / partitions L(x) that are considered. Apart from the constant partition L(x) = 0, all the partitions seem to be largely based off of semantic distinctions that are already available in datasets. (Because of this, I also wondered whether the behavior is largely inherited from the dataset and from the existing semantic structure within it.) Are there more complex L(x), that are not semantically constructed, that can be considered? Separate but related to this, is it valid to consider L(x) that have some knowledge of the distribution D, or even a particular training set? For instance, L(x) that is constructed from trained intermediate layer embeddings in a neural network -- or would L(x) that has knowledge of the distribution D potentially break FCC?

(For instance, this type of L(x) / y constructed from true labels / label noise on existing label structure, e.g. CIFAR-10, is also used in the non-interpolating setting in Section 5.2, which makes the reader wonder what is actually happening in the interpolating setting. More generally, I found Section 5.2 to be in tension with the main message prior to that section, and perhaps too preliminary to be presentable.)

--I think one other experiment that would significantly strengthen the paper is one that tests the implications mentioned for scaling limits in L107 (e.g. measure feature calibration as models are scaled from the underparametered scaling regime to overparameterized.) This would give some more diverse evidence of places where feature calibration breaks down.

Nit points:
--Typo L263: spare -> "sparse"
--Typo L265: Figure label should be "2A"
--L749 in appendix is cut off
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The paper does not address potential negative societal impact, but I believe it is not necessary given the nature of the work.

The authors do provide adequate discussion of limitations and are clear about the conjectural portions of their work.

Perhaps it is worth pointing this out and the connection between the two (e.g. in the setting where p(y|x) is discussed, section starting at L107). 

--In the empirical examples with label noise, why do the authors consider only sparse confusion matrices?

--I think this work has good empirical diversity across very distinct classes of interpolating models, but one place where the paper could be noticeably strengthened is diversity of examples with respect to the datasets / partitions L(x) that are considered. Apart from the constant partition L(x) = 0, all the partitions seem to be largely based off of semantic distinctions that are already available in datasets. (Because of this, I also wondered whether the behavior is largely inherited from the dataset and from the existing semantic structure within it.) Are there more complex L(x), that are not semantically constructed, that can be considered? Separate but related to this, is it valid to consider L(x) that have some knowledge of the distribution D, or even a particular training set? For instance, L(x) that is constructed from trained intermediate layer embeddings in a neural network -- or would L(x) that has knowledge of the distribution D potentially break FCC?

(For instance, this type of L(x) / y constructed from true labels / label noise on existing label structure, e.g. CIFAR-10, is also used in the non-interpolating setting in Section 5.2, which makes the reader wonder what is actually happening in the interpolating setting. More generally, I found Section 5.2 to be in tension with the main message prior to that section, and perhaps too preliminary to be presentable.)

--I think one other experiment that would significantly strengthen the paper is one that tests the implications mentioned for scaling limits in L107 (e.g. measure feature calibration as models are scaled from the underparametered scaling regime to overparameterized.) This would give some more diverse evidence of places where feature calibration breaks down.

Nit points:
--Typo L263: spare -> "sparse"
--Typo L265: Figure label should be "2A"
--L749 in appendix is cut off

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper does not address potential negative societal impact, but I believe it is not necessary given the nature of the work.

The authors do provide adequate discussion of limitations and are clear about the conjectural portions of their work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Distributional Generalization in Interpolating Classifiers

## Summary

This paper introduces the concept of "distributional generalization" and proposes the Feature Calibration Conjecture, which posits that for interpolating classifiers with learnable features L(x), the joint distribution of (L(x), f(x)) matches the joint distribution of (L(x), y). The authors provide theoretical validation for the 1-Nearest Neighbors case and extensive empirical evidence across multiple model classes. The work challenges prevailing narratives around benign overfitting and offers fresh insights into how overparameterized models relate to the underlying data distribution.

## Strengths

The paper makes several notable contributions that warrant publication. First, the authors identify an original empirical property of interpolating classifiers that appears genuinely distinct from existing literature. The Feature Calibration Conjecture represents a bold and conceptually novel direction for learning theory, asking fundamentally new questions about the foundations of generalization. The formulation is both mathematically reasonable and accessible.

The empirical validation is commendable in scope, spanning neural networks, kernel methods, and decision trees across multiple experimental settings. The theoretical contribution, while limited to 1-Nearest Neighbors, provides rigorous support for the main conjecture through Theorem 1. The paper is clearly written and straightforward to follow, with authors demonstrating intellectual honesty by acknowledging limitations and addressing potential concerns thoroughly.

Beyond these direct contributions, the work makes valuable conceptual connections to implicit bias and scaling limits in learning theory. A particularly significant insight is that overparameterized models approach sampling from p(y|x) rather than converging to Bayes optimal classifiers—a finding with important implications for understanding modern machine learning.

## Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, several limitations merit attention. The Feature Calibration Conjecture relies on the notion of "natural distributions," which is not fully specified or formalized. This conceptual ambiguity undermines the precision of the main claim and makes it difficult to assess when the conjecture should or should not apply.

The empirical validation, while spanning multiple model types, shows limited diversity with respect to datasets and partition types L(x). More problematically, all partitions examined beyond the trivial constant partition appear to be based on semantic distinctions already present in the datasets. This raises a critical question: is the observed behavior genuinely a property of the models, or is it primarily inherited from the structure of the data itself? The paper does not adequately disentangle these possibilities.

The work would benefit from exploring more complex, non-semantically-constructed partitions. Additionally, the paper lacks experiments testing the scaling limit implications discussed theoretically—specifically, how feature calibration evolves as models transition from underparameterized to overparameterized regimes. This is a missed opportunity to strengthen the main narrative.

Section 5.2's treatment of non-interpolating methods is notably preliminary and creates tension with the otherwise rigorous main message. The confusion matrices considered in label noise experiments are restricted to sparse cases, limiting the generality of conclusions about noise robustness.

Finally, there are occasional lapses in clarity regarding the definition and usage of "Bayes optimal classifier," particularly in distinguishing between argmax p(y|L(x)) and argmax p(y|x).

## Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen the work, I recommend:

1. **Clarify terminology**: Provide precise definitions of "Bayes optimal classifier" and explicitly distinguish between conditioning on L(x) versus x throughout the paper.

2. **Expand empirical scope**: Investigate more diverse partitions, particularly those that are not semantically constructed and that do not rely on knowledge of the training distribution or dataset structure.

3. **Test scaling behavior**: Add experiments measuring feature calibration across the underparameterized-to-overparameterized transition to directly validate the scaling limit implications.

4. **Strengthen or remove Section 5.2**: Either develop a more rigorous treatment of non-interpolating methods or consider removing this section, as its preliminary nature detracts from the main narrative.

5. **Increase experimental diversity**: Expand the range of datasets and partition types to provide more comprehensive empirical validation.

6. **Generalize noise experiments**: Consider non-sparse confusion matrices in label noise experiments to assess robustness more broadly.

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents original ideas executed with care and rigor. The Feature Calibration Conjecture is conceptually novel and challenges important assumptions in learning theory. While the theoretical results are limited to a specific case and several empirical questions remain about the source of observed behavior, the work makes sufficient contributions to warrant publication. The clear presentation, honest acknowledgment of limitations, and potential impact on future research in understanding interpolation and generalization support acceptance.

The paper would be strengthened by addressing the suggestions above, particularly in clarifying the notion of "natural distributions," expanding empirical scope, and better disentangling model properties from data properties. Nevertheless, the work represents a solid contribution to an important area of learning theory.

**Recommendation: Accept**
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This submission proposes a new type of generalization satisfied by interpolating classifiers (the primary focus), that is different from agreement between test error and train error as in classical learning theory. This type of generalization is largely motivated by empirical evidence across different models (neural networks, kernel regression & svm, decision trees) and theoretically for 1-Nearest Neighbors classification (Theorem 1). More specifically, the submission claims that for certain types of mappings x -> L(x) (termed "distinguishable features" -- i.e. they must be learnable to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration), the joint distribution of (L(x), y) where (x,y) pairs are drawn from the true distribution will be "close" to the joint distribution of (L(x), f(x)) on the test set, where f(x) is the trained model prediction.

List of contributions:
--Proposes that interpolating classifiers satisfy "distributional generalization." This is first more specifically formulated as the "Feature Calibration Conjecture" (FCC) in Section 3. A crucial component of this is to identify the types of features L(x) for which the calibration holds (Definition 1); roughly, if the feature is learnable from inputs x to good test accuracy in the setting under consideration (fixed algorithm, data distribution, number of samples, allowable error), then under that same setting calibration will be achieved. The authors prove FCC is true for 1-Nearest neighbors. The Feature Calibration Conjecture is not fully specified, in that there is an assumption of "natural distribution" D (which is such that the conjecture holds in the empirical settings considered).

--Empirical test of the FCC. The types of distinguishable features L considered include (i) the constant partition (calibration then implies the marginal distribution p(y) of the predicted labels matches that of the true label distribution); (ii) a "coarse partition" (L(x) is an "easy" classification task) and calibration holds for fine-grained label subgroups, for instance in Imagenet; (iii) "class partition" (classes that can be learned well from standard datasets, and the true label distribution is a noisy corruption of this); (iv) multiple features. Models investigated include neural networks (WideResnets and MLPs), kernels (standard and high-performing ones), and decision trees. 

--A definition for "distributional generalization" which subsumes the definition of feature calibration & classical generalization, and very preliminary evidence that this holds for non-interpolating models (a Wide Resnet model on CIFAR-10 during training).

Main Review:
Originality: This work identifies & investigates an original empirical property that appears to be satisfied by interpolating classifiers. The proposed concepts (Feature Calibration) are general & seem distinct from any prior work to the best of my knowledge. (For example, the authors note that "standard" calibration is a particular case of Feature Calibration with specific choice of L(x).) I think this work is bold in what it seeks to accomplish (asking a fundamentally new question at the basis of learning theory, building evidence from diverse empirical examples) and makes a reasonable first step towards doing so. 

Quality: The quality of the work seems high. The authors give a number of different empirical examples with sufficient coverage across model diversity and provide a theoretically satisfied case (1-Nearest Neighbors). The authors note the limitations of the work (e.g. the FCC is specified in terms of "natural distributions" which has an unclear meaning) and are fairly thorough in addressing potential concerns & connections to other works. I was not able to identify significant shortcomings with respect to the conceptual formulation or message of the paper. I make some comments about diversity of empirical examples (with respect to datasets / partitions) below.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and was straightforward to read.

Significance: I think this paper takes a risk in its premise, provides a reasonable amount of evidence in support of its main claims, and would spur further work in this area; as I was also not able to identify any major issues with the paper, this is the reason behind my suggested score. In Section 1.3 the authors discuss a number of consequences for learning theory which seem notable. This work tells us more about the implicit bias of models (though not just neural networks); most interesting I think is that in certain limits (e.g. when models are interpolating, and model size >> dataset size), models are not approaching the Bayes optimal classifier but sample from the distribution p(y|x). 

Other comments & questions:

--I find some mentions of "Bayes optimal classifier" to be confusing at times -- for instance, its usage in L268 seems to be with respect to argmax p(y | L(x)) (rather than argmax p(y|x)), if I understand correctly. Perhaps it is worth pointing this out and the connection between the two (e.g. in the setting where p(y|x) is discussed, section starting at L107). 

--In the empirical examples with label noise, why do the authors consider only sparse confusion matrices?

--I think this work has good empirical diversity across very distinct classes of interpolating models, but one place where the paper could be noticeably strengthened is diversity of examples with respect to the datasets / partitions L(x) that are considered. Apart from the constant partition L(x) = 0, all the partitions seem to be largely based off of semantic distinctions that are already available in datasets. (Because of this, I also wondered whether the behavior is largely inherited from the dataset and from the existing semantic structure within it.) Are there more complex L(x), that are not semantically constructed, that can be considered? Separate but related to this, is it valid to consider L(x) that have some knowledge of the distribution D, or even a particular training set? For instance, L(x) that is constructed from trained intermediate layer embeddings in a neural network -- or would L(x) that has knowledge of the distribution D potentially break FCC?

(For instance, this type of L(x) / y constructed from true labels / label noise on existing label structure, e.g. CIFAR-10, is also used in the non-interpolating setting in Section 5.2, which makes the reader wonder what is actually happening in the interpolating setting. More generally, I found Section 5.2 to be in tension with the main message prior to that section, and perhaps too preliminary to be presentable.)

--I think one other experiment that would significantly strengthen the paper is one that tests the implications mentioned for scaling limits in L107 (e.g. measure feature calibration as models are scaled from the underparametered scaling regime to overparameterized.) This would give some more diverse evidence of places where feature calibration breaks down.

Nit points:
--Typo L263: spare -> "sparse"
--Typo L265: Figure label should be "2A"
--L749 in appendix is cut off

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper does not address potential negative societal impact, but I believe it is not necessary given the nature of the work.

The authors do provide adequate discussion of limitations and are clear about the conjectural portions of their work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

Definition 1 formalizes distinguishable features as partitions L where training achieves test accuracy ≥ 1-ε
Theorem 1 proves Feature Calibration for 1-Nearest Neighbors under regularity condition that nearest-neighbor sampling yields approximately uniform test points
Distributional closeness measured using TV-distance between joint distributions
Experiments test multiple partition types: constant (L(x)=0), coarse (ImageNet dogs), class-based, and multiple features (CelebA)
TV-distance between (L(x), f(x)) and (L(x), y) is upper bounded by ε, the error of learning L
Non-interpolating methods discussed informally in Section 5.2 but lack rigorous treatment
Regularity assumption in Theorem 1 requires NNS(x) distribution to be close to uniform over test set

reviews.summary: The authors consider interpolating classifiers, i.e. classifiers that interpolate the training data by selecting the correct class for training samples, often performing poorly in terms of generalization error. The authors propose a new notion of generalization, i.e. the Distributional Generalization. Such a notion does not focus on the average error over the inputs, and subsumes both classical generalization and the conjecture that the outputs of classifiers match the statistics of their training distribution when conditioned on certain subgroups. The authors formally introduce and experimentally validate a conjecture to specify such subgroups. While the conjecture is proven for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, empirical evidence is provided for other classifiers; such as kernel machines and neural networks.
reviews.main_review:
== Main comments ==

- The paper is overall well written and the topics are clearly exposed. Illustrative examples are effectively presented to further clarify the problem at hand.
- The presented work tackles a timely and relevant problem in machine learning, drawing ideas from many related areas of research, such as output distribution calibration, overparametrization, and benign overfitting. The presented results are worthy but not completely surprising. 
- The paper addresses a very much similar problem than : 
Vapnik, V. & Izmailov, R.. (2020). Complete statistical theory of learning: learning using statistical invariants. Proceedings of the Ninth Symposium on Conformal and Probabilistic Prediction and Applications, in PMLR 128:4-40
However, this problem is known to below to the class of  ill-posed problems. How does the current work related to the above mentioned reference? How does it overcome the ill-posed problem. 
- In eq. (1) the notation is a bit confusing. Are TrainSet and TestSet the distributions from which the samples of these sets are drawn (but then D_tr, D_te are used to mean the same), or rather the sets themselves (and then ~ should be replaced with ∈)?
- One of the two main contributions is thoroughly expressed in section 3.2. This is indeed a very interesting observation. However it looks closely related to the problem defined in section 2 of [26].
Could the authors further underline the value and originality of their contribution by discussing the connection between the two concepts?
- Although the feature calibration conjecture is fully proved to 1-nearest-neighbors classifiers, it is also true that it represents a very limited model. It seems that for more complex (and more commonly used) models, only empirical evidence can be provided so far, with no much theoretical formalization. Although this is not necessarily a negative aspect for works in progress and contributions which bring new ideas, the expectation set up by the writers in the introductory section are diminished by lack of formalization.
- The training procedure is sometimes indicated as Train_A, and sometimes as A. We suggest to choose one symbol and consistently use it throughout the whole paper.
- Have the authors thought of any potential issue related to identifying potentially sensitive subgroups L such as, ethnicity, high/low income, etc. instead of dogs, cats, etc. when using different
datasets?
- We would like to draw attention on the fact that, in line 101, the reference to the work in [7] does not seem to properly fit it. Indeed, in [7] the authors already explain that ‘benign overfitting’ is only characterized in specific cases and does not generally apply, making the claim in lines 103-104 appear a bit excessive. Reference [26] should be reported as the version officially published at ICML 2017.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The authors take into the scientific limitations of their work in section 3.4. Further improvements can be implemented by answering the questions and doubts raised in the Main Review.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors consider interpolating classifiers, i.e. classifiers that interpolate the training data by selecting the correct class for training samples, often performing poorly in terms of generalization error. The authors propose a new notion of generalization, i.e. the Distributional Generalization. Such a notion does not focus on the average error over the inputs, and subsumes both classical generalization and the conjecture that the outputs of classifiers match the statistics of their training distribution when conditioned on certain subgroups. The authors formally introduce and experimentally validate a conjecture to specify such subgroups. While the conjecture is proven for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, empirical evidence is provided for other classifiers; such as kernel machines and neural networks.

Main Review:
== Main comments ==

- The paper is overall well written and the topics are clearly exposed. Illustrative examples are effectively presented to further clarify the problem at hand.
- The presented work tackles a timely and relevant problem in machine learning, drawing ideas from many related areas of research, such as output distribution calibration, overparametrization, and benign overfitting. The presented results are worthy but not completely surprising. 
- The paper addresses a very much similar problem than : 
Vapnik, V. & Izmailov, R.. (2020). Complete statistical theory of learning: learning using statistical invariants. Proceedings of the Ninth Symposium on Conformal and Probabilistic Prediction and Applications, in PMLR 128:4-40
However, this problem is known to below to the class of  ill-posed problems. How does the current work related to the above mentioned reference? How does it overcome the ill-posed problem. 
- In eq. (1) the notation is a bit confusing. Are TrainSet and TestSet the distributions from which the samples of these sets are drawn (but then D_tr, D_te are used to mean the same), or rather the sets themselves (and then ~ should be replaced with ∈)?
- One of the two main contributions is thoroughly expressed in section 3.2. This is indeed a very interesting observation. However it looks closely related to the problem defined in section 2 of [26].
Could the authors further underline the value and originality of their contribution by discussing the connection between the two concepts?
- Although the feature calibration conjecture is fully proved to 1-nearest-neighbors classifiers, it is also true that it represents a very limited model. It seems that for more complex (and more commonly used) models, only empirical evidence can be provided so far, with no much theoretical formalization. Although this is not necessarily a negative aspect for works in progress and contributions which bring new ideas, the expectation set up by the writers in the introductory section are diminished by lack of formalization.
- The training procedure is sometimes indicated as Train_A, and sometimes as A. We suggest to choose one symbol and consistently use it throughout the whole paper.
- Have the authors thought of any potential issue related to identifying potentially sensitive subgroups L such as, ethnicity, high/low income, etc. instead of dogs, cats, etc. when using different
datasets?
- We would like to draw attention on the fact that, in line 101, the reference to the work in [7] does not seem to properly fit it. Indeed, in [7] the authors already explain that ‘benign overfitting’ is only characterized in specific cases and does not generally apply, making the claim in lines 103-104 appear a bit excessive. Reference [26] should be reported as the version officially published at ICML 2017.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors take into the scientific limitations of their work in section 3.4. Further improvements can be implemented by answering the questions and doubts raised in the Main Review.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Distributional Generalization for Interpolating Classifiers

## Summary

This paper investigates distributional generalization in interpolating classifiers, proposing a Feature Calibration Conjecture to explain why classifiers that perfectly fit training data can still generalize well. While the work addresses a timely problem and is generally well-written with effective illustrations, it suffers from significant limitations in theoretical scope and insufficient differentiation from related work. The main contribution—formal proof of the conjecture—applies only to 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, while practical deep learning models rely solely on empirical validation without theoretical grounding.

## Strengths

The paper is clearly written with well-motivated examples that effectively communicate the central problem. The authors tackle a relevant question in modern machine learning, particularly given the prevalence of overparameterized models that interpolate training data yet generalize well. The work thoughtfully synthesizes ideas from multiple research areas including output calibration, overparameterization, and benign overfitting.

The experimental validation is commendable, spanning diverse classifier architectures including neural networks, kernel machines, and decision trees. Theorem 1 provides a rigorous proof for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers under mild regularity conditions, demonstrating technical competence.

## Major Weaknesses

### Limited Theoretical Scope

The most significant limitation is the restriction of formal analysis to 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers. While this provides theoretical rigor for a simple model, it severely limits the contribution's relevance to practical machine learning. The paper's introduction sets expectations for understanding modern classifiers, but these expectations are undermined by the lack of theoretical formalization for the complex models that actually motivated the research. For neural networks, kernel machines, and other commonly used approaches, only empirical evidence is presented.

### Insufficient Engagement with Related Work

The paper fails to adequately address its relationship to closely related work. Most critically, the connection to Vapnik & Izmailov (2020) on statistical invariants is not discussed, leaving unclear how the current work overcomes the ill-posed problem nature inherent in that formulation. Additionally, the Feature Calibration Conjecture appears closely related to concepts already defined in reference [26] (ICML 2017). The paper does not sufficiently clarify the originality and added value of the current contribution relative to this prior work. This is a substantial concern for assessing novelty.

### Limited Novelty

The core results, while technically sound, are not particularly surprising. The finding that classifiers can generalize despite perfect training fit aligns with existing understanding from benign overfitting literature and related work on calibration. Without clearer differentiation from prior art, the contribution appears incremental.

## Technical Issues

**Notation and Clarity**: Equation (1) uses confusing notation regarding whether TrainSet and TestSet refer to probability distributions or empirical sets. This ambiguity should be resolved explicitly. Additionally, the training procedure is inconsistently denoted as both Train_A and A throughout the paper; consistent notation is necessary.

**Definition Concerns**: The Feature Calibration Conjecture involves multiple quantifiers over (ε, A, D, n) whose specific choices may appear arbitrary. Moreover, Definition 1 (Distinguishable Feature) depends on the marginal distribution p(x) but not on the conditional p(y|x), which warrants clearer justification.

**Reference Issues**: Reference [26] should cite the officially published ICML 2017 version rather than a pre-publication form. More importantly, the citation to reference [7] on benign overfitting does not accurately represent that work's claims. The benign overfitting phenomenon, as established in prior literature, applies only under specific conditions, not as a general explanation for the observed behavior.

## Missing Discussions

The paper does not address potential practical challenges when applying this framework beyond controlled settings like animal/object classification. Specifically, identifying and handling sensitive demographic subgroups (ethnicity, income, etc.) when measuring distributional differences could raise important fairness concerns that deserve discussion.

## Minor Suggestions

- Clarify the relationship between the Feature Calibration Conjecture and calibration concepts in reference [26], explicitly stating what is novel
- Provide a detailed discussion of how this work relates to and extends Vapnik & Izmailov (2020)
- Acknowledge the limitation of theoretical results to 1-nearest-neighbor as a significant constraint on the contribution's scope
- Correct the characterization of benign overfitting to align with established results in the literature
- Consider discussing the applicability and limitations of the framework for real-world datasets with sensitive attributes

## Recommendation

This paper makes a reasonable but ultimately insufficient contribution to warrant acceptance. While the writing is clear and the problem is relevant, the theoretical results are restricted to an overly simple model that bears limited connection to modern machine learning practice. The empirical validation of the conjecture for practical classifiers, though comprehensive, cannot substitute for theoretical understanding. Most critically, the paper does not adequately position itself relative to closely related prior work, raising concerns about novelty and originality. The work would benefit from either extending theoretical results to more general model classes or providing a more thorough engagement with related literature to establish clear points of distinction.

**Decision: Reject**

The paper presents competent work on a timely problem but falls short of the acceptance threshold due to limited theoretical scope, insufficient novelty, and inadequate discussion of related work.

Comprehensive experimental validation across multiple classifier types (neural networks, kernel machines, decision trees)

Work addresses a very similar problem to Vapnik & Izmailov (2020) but fails to discuss the connection or how it overcomes the ill-posed problem nature of that work
Feature Calibration Conjecture closely related to problem defined in section 2 of reference [26], with insufficient discussion of originality and value compared to that work
Conjecture is only formally proven for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, which is a very limited model
For more complex and commonly used models, only empirical evidence is provided with no theoretical formalization
Notation in Equation (1) is confusing regarding whether TrainSet and TestSet refer to distributions or sets themselves
Inconsistent notation: training procedure sometimes indicated as Train_A and sometimes as A
Reference [26] should be reported as officially published at ICML 2017, not in its pre-publication form
Reference [7] (benign overfitting) does not properly support claims in lines 103-104, as that work already explains benign overfitting only applies in specific cases
suggestions: Discuss connection to Vapnik & Izmailov (2020) work on statistical invariants and explain how current work overcomes the ill-posed nature of that problem
Clarify the relationship between Feature Calibration Conjecture and the calibration concept in reference [26], explicitly underline value and originality of contribution
Provide theoretical formalization for classifiers beyond 1-nearest-neighbor, or acknowledge this as a significant limitation
Clarify notation in Equation (1) to distinguish whether TrainSet and TestSet refer to distributions or sets
Consistently use either Train_A or A notation for training procedure throughout the paper
Update reference [26] to cite the officially published ICML 2017 version
Correct the claim about benign overfitting in reference [7] to accurately reflect what that work states
Address potential issues related to identifying sensitive subgroups (ethnicity, income, etc.) when applying the framework to different datasets beyond animal/object classification
main_points: The conjecture is only formally proven for 1-nearest-neighbor classifiers, a very limited model
Empirical evidence for more complex models lacks theoretical formalization
Insufficient discussion of relationship to prior work, particularly Vapnik & Izmailov (2020) and reference [26]
Expectations set in introduction are diminished by lack of formalization for practical classifiers
No discussion of potential issues with identifying sensitive demographic subgroups using this framework
Novelty and originality of contribution not sufficiently distinguished from related work
technical_details: Notation confusion in Equation (1) regarding TrainSet/TestSet as distributions versus sets
Inconsistent use of Train_A versus A for training procedure notation
Feature Calibration Conjecture definition depends on many quantifiers over (ε, A, D, n) which may seem arbitrary
Definition 1 (Distinguishable Feature) depends on marginal distribution of x, not on label distribution p(y|x)
Proof of Theorem 1 is straightforward but applies only to 1-nearest-neighbor under mild regularity conditions
Distributional closeness measured using TV-distance and test functions framework

reviews.summary: The paper studies a federated learning scheme for training machine learning models using gradients provided by a set of strategic users. At each iteration, each user gets a mini-batch of samples, computes her gradient, and decides whether to report it to the fusion center (FC) or to report a random signal.  The authors propose a strategy for the FC which incentivizes users to truthfully report their gradients and ensure convergence guarantees. Results are validated experimentally on publicly available datasets.
reviews.main_review:
Federated learning is a relevant problem in machine learning and I believe this work positively contributes to the existing literature. 
The work adds both novel theoretical contributions and experimental results. Moreover, it is clearly written. Hence, my acceptance score.  However, I have a few comments for the authors: 

- It would be nice to have related works discussed in the main paper, clearly highlighting the differences in terms of set-ups and assumptions considered. I believe the problem of federated learning can be tackled from a lot of different perspectives and it would be nice to have this clear from the first sections. A lot of technical definitions and details could be moved to the Appendix instead (e.g., Equation 20).

- In the experiments, only a static strategy vector is considered for the users. However, it would be natural to consider users who adapt their strategy online. Is there a particular reason why this was not considered? This would test whether the FC algorithm actually steers the agents to collaborate in the long run.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Game-Theoretic Incentive Mechanisms in Federated Learning

## Summary

This paper presents a novel approach to addressing a significant challenge in federated learning: incentivizing strategic, self-interested users to report truthful gradients and maintain cooperation. The authors employ game-theoretic mechanisms, specifically zero-determinant (ZD) strategies, to design incentive structures for a fusion center that encourage user collaboration. The work contributes both theoretical convergence guarantees and empirical validation of the proposed approach. While the paper makes a solid contribution to the federated learning literature, there are several presentation and experimental concerns that limit its impact.

## Strengths

1. **Relevant Problem Formulation**: The paper tackles a practical and important challenge in federated learning—how to incentivize cooperation among strategic users. This is an underexplored yet critical aspect of real-world distributed learning systems.

2. **Theoretical Rigor**: The authors provide formal convergence guarantees for their proposed algorithm, accounting for classification errors and their impact on the repeated game dynamics. This theoretical foundation strengthens the contribution.

3. **Integrated Approach**: The combination of game theory with federated learning is well-motivated and novel. The proposed gradient estimation and user action classification scheme provides a practical mechanism for the fusion center to operate without directly observing user actions.

4. **Clear Presentation**: The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow, making the technical content accessible to readers.

5. **Complete Validation**: The work includes both theoretical analysis and experimental validation on standard datasets (KDD-Cup 04 and MNIST), demonstrating the practical applicability of the approach.

## Weaknesses

1. **Positioning and Related Work**: The related work discussion is placed in the appendix, which significantly weakens the paper's positioning. The main text does not clearly articulate how this work differs from existing federated learning approaches or what novel assumptions and setups distinguish this contribution. Readers cannot easily understand the novelty without consulting the appendix.

2. **Suboptimal Main Paper Organization**: The inclusion of technical definitions and equations (e.g., Equation 20) that could reside in the appendix creates unnecessary clutter in the main narrative. This space could be better utilized for discussing related work and positioning the contribution.

3. **Limited Experimental Scope**: The experiments only evaluate the algorithm against users employing static strategy vectors. This is a significant limitation because it does not test whether the proposed incentive mechanism actually steers adaptive, strategic users toward cooperation in practice. Real users would likely adapt their strategies in response to the fusion center's actions.

4. **Incomplete Validation of Core Claims**: While the paper claims to incentivize long-term cooperation, there is no experimental evidence that the fusion center's algorithm successfully achieves this goal when users adapt their strategies dynamically. This represents a gap between the theoretical motivation and empirical validation.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Reorganize Related Work**: Move the related work discussion to the main paper and explicitly highlight how the problem setup, assumptions, and technical approach differ from prior work in federated learning. This will clarify the paper's contributions and position it appropriately within the literature.

2. **Streamline Main Paper Content**: Consider relocating technical definitions and proofs (such as Equation 20 and supporting derivations) to the appendix to improve readability and flow in the main narrative.

3. **Extend Experimental Evaluation**: Conduct experiments where users employ adaptive strategies that evolve over time in response to the fusion center's actions. This would provide more realistic validation of the algorithm's ability to incentivize cooperation.

4. **Test Long-term Cooperation**: Design experiments specifically to evaluate whether the proposed mechanism successfully steers users toward sustained cooperation, including metrics that measure the stability and robustness of cooperation over extended interactions.

## Minor Comments

- The experimental setup with K=50 users and mini-batch size s=10 is reasonable, but discussing the sensitivity of results to these parameters would strengthen the evaluation.
- The paper would benefit from discussing computational complexity and scalability implications of the proposed approach.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a meaningful contribution to federated learning by introducing game-theoretic mechanisms to address the challenge of incentivizing strategic users. The theoretical analysis is sound, and the general approach is novel and well-motivated. However, the presentation could be significantly improved by moving related work to the main paper and clarifying the contribution's novelty. More importantly, the experimental validation is limited by the assumption of static user strategies, which does not adequately test the algorithm's effectiveness in realistic scenarios where users adapt their behavior.

Despite these limitations, the work presents sufficient novelty and rigor to merit publication. The core ideas are valuable, and with the suggested revisions—particularly regarding presentation and more comprehensive experiments with adaptive users—this paper would make a stronger contribution to the field.

**Recommendation: Marginally Above Acceptance Threshold**

The paper should be accepted, but the authors are strongly encouraged to address the organizational and experimental limitations in a revision to enhance the work's impact and clarity.

reviews.summary: The paper formulates the interaction between the fusion center (FC) and users in distributed learning (or federated learning) from the perspective of game theory. In order to encourage the users to provide effective information to the FC for model update, the FC adopt zero-determinant (ZD) strategy, associated with which there are some practical issues addressed by the authors. Theoretical analysis on the convergence of the proposed algorithm is presented.
reviews.main_review:
The paper formulates the interaction between the fusion center (FC) and users in distributed learning (or federated learning) from the perspective of game theory. In order to encourage the users to provide effective information to the FC for model update, the FC adopt zero-determinant (ZD) strategy, associated with which there are some practical issues addressed by the authors. Theoretical analysis on the convergence of the proposed algorithm is presented. The formulation and the results are interesting; however, there are some concerns from the reviewer:

1.	What’s the motivation of the users submitting random noninformative signal to the FC instead of just submitting nothing when they decide not to cooperate. Submitting random noninformative signal does not benefit the users but harms the FC. 
2.	Please explain the state transition matrix $\Omega$ in eq. (3), why it is constructed as it is and why the states are supposed to evolve according to it. Just below eq. (3), the authors use \bar{\Omega} to denote the transition matrix, what is the difference between $\Omega$ and $\bar{\Omega}$, or it is just a typo?
3.	The authors are expected to clearly state the goal of users in playing the game, namely, in each iteration, the users maximize what, the expected long-term reward, or the reward of this iteration. Based on the objective of the users, the author shall show that the users’ best response is q* = [1, 1, 1, 1]^T given that the FC adopts ZD strategy. 
4.	In the Experiments section, the figure referred is Fig 2 rather than Fig 4. SGSU is SGD-SU, typo? Does Pavlov mean the win-stay-lose-switch? If it does, please just use one of these two phrases throughout the paper. 
5.	From Figure 2(a) and (b), it is shown that the optimality gap is the smallest when the users take tit-for-tat strategy. Does this mean the FC prefers tit-for-tat instead of full-cooperation in order to obtain a favorable solution to optimization problem (1)? This is not consistent with the game, where the FC prefers full-cooperation? 
6.	Since the problem is formulated as a repeated game, the experiments shall show whether the strategy of the users converges exactly to q* starting from any initial state and action. 
7.	The reviewer believes (24) admits a closed-form solution, and the computational complexity of (24) is the same as that of (6). Please check this.
