reviews.summary: This paper proposed two shuffling based stochastic optimization methods, ProxRR and FedRR. ProxRR is the proximal version of SGD with shuffling, and they prove that ProxRR achieves better convergence rate than vanilla proximal SGD on strongly convex function, similar to its counterpart SGD with shuffling. Another advantage of this paper is that their convergence results do not need bounded gradient assumption, which was needed in the several prior shuffling SGD papers. In FedRR algorithm, each client uses SGD without replacement to optimize the local model, instead of vanilla SGD employed in local SGD. They also have analysis of FedRR for convex and nonconvex functions under both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings, and the convergence rates are faster than local SGD. The experiments also show the faster convergence of shuffling based algorithms.
reviews.main_review: As far as I know, this paper is the first to analyze the convergence of proximal SGD without replacement, and it is also the first to introduce shuffling ideas into federated optimization. The theoretical analysis is sound to me. The experiments also support their theory. The paper is well-written and both experimental and theoretical results are clearly presented. This paper introduces shuffled local updates in local SGD, which is a well-known idea to speedup SGD in the traditional optimization community. As the authors showed in the paper, this idea does improve the convergence rate of vanilla local SGD, and the experiments also support that.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1. For ProxRR, I am curious about the comparison with the literature of shuffling SGD. Particularly, [1] gives a lower bound $O(n/T^2)$, so can we choose the learning rate carefully such that we can match with this lower bound? 

2.  It is a decent algorithm and it does provably improve local SGD. My minor concern is that FedRR is a simple combination of local SGD and SGD without replacement. It would be great if we can apply the shuffling idea on client level as well, e.g., for model averaging stage, server does not randomly sample a subset of clients to do the average, instead it shuffles the clients and selects subsets without replacement. It makes the algorithm look more tailored for federated learning. 

[1] Rajput, Shashank, Anant Gupta, and Dimitris Papailiopoulos. "Closing the convergence gap of SGD without replacement." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.

reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposed two shuffling based stochastic optimization methods, ProxRR and FedRR. ProxRR is the proximal version of SGD with shuffling, and they prove that ProxRR achieves better convergence rate than vanilla proximal SGD on strongly convex function, similar to its counterpart SGD with shuffling. Another advantage of this paper is that their convergence results do not need bounded gradient assumption, which was needed in the several prior shuffling SGD papers. In FedRR algorithm, each client uses SGD without replacement to optimize the local model, instead of vanilla SGD employed in local SGD. They also have analysis of FedRR for convex and nonconvex functions under both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings, and the convergence rates are faster than local SGD. The experiments also show the faster convergence of shuffling based algorithms.

Main Review:
As far as I know, this paper is the first to analyze the convergence of proximal SGD without replacement, and it is also the first to introduce shuffling ideas into federated optimization. The theoretical analysis is sound to me. The experiments also support their theory. The paper is well-written and both experimental and theoretical results are clearly presented. This paper introduces shuffled local updates in local SGD, which is a well-known idea to speedup SGD in the traditional optimization community. As the authors showed in the paper, this idea does improve the convergence rate of vanilla local SGD, and the experiments also support that.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
1. For ProxRR, I am curious about the comparison with the literature of shuffling SGD. Particularly, [1] gives a lower bound $O(n/T^2)$, so can we choose the learning rate carefully such that we can match with this lower bound? 

2.  It is a decent algorithm and it does provably improve local SGD. My minor concern is that FedRR is a simple combination of local SGD and SGD without replacement. It would be great if we can apply the shuffling idea on client level as well, e.g., for model averaging stage, server does not randomly sample a subset of clients to do the average, instead it shuffles the clients and selects subsets without replacement. It makes the algorithm look more tailored for federated learning. 

[1] Rajput, Shashank, Anant Gupta, and Dimitris Papailiopoulos. "Closing the convergence gap of SGD without replacement." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: ProxRR and FedRR - Proximal and Federated Random Reshuffling Methods

## Summary

This paper introduces ProxRR and FedRR, extending random reshuffling optimization methods to proximal settings and federated learning contexts. The work provides theoretical convergence analysis for both variants and validates the findings experimentally. While the paper makes meaningful contributions to the optimization literature, particularly in extending shuffling-based methods to new problem classes, there are concerns regarding the tightness of the theoretical results and the sophistication of the federated approach that limit its impact.

## Strengths

The paper makes several notable contributions to the field. Most significantly, this appears to be the first rigorous analysis of proximal SGD without replacement, filling an important gap in the literature. The extension of shuffling ideas to federated optimization is also novel and timely. The theoretical analysis is sound and demonstrates careful technical work across multiple settings.

A particularly valuable aspect of the theoretical contribution is the removal of bounded gradient assumptions, which have been required in several prior analyses of shuffling-based SGD methods. This relaxation strengthens the applicability of the results. The convergence analysis is comprehensive, covering both strongly convex and nonconvex regimes, as well as both homogeneous and heterogeneous data distributions in federated settings. This breadth demonstrates thorough theoretical treatment.

The experimental validation is well-executed and supports the theoretical predictions. Additionally, the paper is clearly written with results presented in an accessible manner. The demonstrated improvement of FedRR over vanilla local SGD provides practical value.

## Weaknesses

Despite these strengths, several limitations warrant discussion. The most significant concern is whether the convergence rates achieved actually match known lower bounds from the literature. Specifically, the O(n/T²) lower bound established by prior work (e.g., Rajput et al., 2020) appears relevant for comparison, yet the paper does not clearly establish whether carefully chosen learning rates can match this bound. This raises questions about the tightness of the analysis and whether the theoretical guarantees are optimal.

The FedRR algorithm, while showing improvements, represents a relatively straightforward combination of local SGD with SGD without replacement. The approach lacks sophistication tailored specifically to the federated learning setting. The algorithm essentially applies shuffling ideas at the client level without fundamentally rethinking how shuffling could be leveraged more creatively in federated contexts. For instance, the algorithm does not explore shuffling at the server level during client selection or model averaging stages, which could represent a more substantial algorithmic contribution.

The federated variant feels somewhat incremental—it adapts existing techniques rather than proposing methods specifically designed with federated challenges in mind. This limits the novelty and impact of the FedRR contribution.

## Suggestions for Improvement

To strengthen the paper, I recommend the following:

1. **Lower Bound Analysis**: Conduct a detailed comparison between the achieved convergence rates and the O(n/T²) lower bound from prior work. Explicitly discuss whether your learning rate choices enable matching these bounds, or characterize any remaining gap. This would clarify the theoretical contributions and their optimality.

2. **Federated Algorithm Design**: Consider extending the shuffling paradigm to client-level selection in federated learning. Rather than random client sampling at each round, the server could shuffle the client population and select subsets without replacement during the model averaging stage. This would represent a more fundamental adaptation of shuffling ideas to federated optimization and could yield more substantial improvements.

3. **Algorithm Sophistication**: Develop FedRR further to be more explicitly tailored to federated learning challenges, such as communication efficiency, client heterogeneity, or stragglers. The current approach feels like a generic application of shuffling rather than a method designed with federated considerations at its core.

## Minor Comments

The paper would benefit from more discussion of computational costs and practical implementation considerations for the federated setting. Additionally, more extensive experiments comparing against other shuffling-based and federated optimization methods would strengthen the empirical validation.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes solid theoretical and practical contributions to optimization for machine learning. The analysis of proximal SGD without replacement is novel and valuable, and the extension to federated learning addresses a relevant problem. The removal of bounded gradient assumptions and comprehensive convergence analysis across multiple settings demonstrate careful theoretical work. However, the federated contribution feels incremental, combining existing ideas without substantial innovation specific to federated contexts. More critically, the paper does not adequately address whether the theoretical results match known lower bounds, leaving questions about optimality.

The work is technically sound and makes contributions worthy of publication, but the limitations prevent it from being a strong accept. The paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold—it advances the field meaningfully while leaving room for more substantial future work.

**Recommendation: Accept (6/10 - Marginally above acceptance threshold)**

Summary:
This paper proposed two shuffling based stochastic optimization methods, ProxRR and FedRR. ProxRR is the proximal version of SGD with shuffling, and they prove that ProxRR achieves better convergence rate than vanilla proximal SGD on strongly convex function, similar to its counterpart SGD with shuffling. Another advantage of this paper is that their convergence results do not need bounded gradient assumption, which was needed in the several prior shuffling SGD papers. In FedRR algorithm, each client uses SGD without replacement to optimize the local model, instead of vanilla SGD employed in local SGD. They also have analysis of FedRR for convex and nonconvex functions under both homogeneous and heterogeneous settings, and the convergence rates are faster than local SGD. The experiments also show the faster convergence of shuffling based algorithms.

Main Review:
As far as I know, this paper is the first to analyze the convergence of proximal SGD without replacement, and it is also the first to introduce shuffling ideas into federated optimization. The theoretical analysis is sound to me. The experiments also support their theory. The paper is well-written and both experimental and theoretical results are clearly presented. This paper introduces shuffled local updates in local SGD, which is a well-known idea to speedup SGD in the traditional optimization community. As the authors showed in the paper, this idea does improve the convergence rate of vanilla local SGD, and the experiments also support that.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
1. For ProxRR, I am curious about the comparison with the literature of shuffling SGD. Particularly, [1] gives a lower bound $O(n/T^2)$, so can we choose the learning rate carefully such that we can match with this lower bound? 

2.  It is a decent algorithm and it does provably improve local SGD. My minor concern is that FedRR is a simple combination of local SGD and SGD without replacement. It would be great if we can apply the shuffling idea on client level as well, e.g., for model averaging stage, server does not randomly sample a subset of clients to do the average, instead it shuffles the clients and selects subsets without replacement. It makes the algorithm look more tailored for federated learning. 

[1] Rajput, Shashank, Anant Gupta, and Dimitris Papailiopoulos. "Closing the convergence gap of SGD without replacement." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.main_review:
In extending random reshuffling methods to composite optimization, the authors observe the importance of only applying the proximal operator once every epoch of random reshuffling, rather than at the end of each step. Thus, their algorithm only evaluates the proximal operator a single time during each pass of the data. They go on to show that their results apply to the setting of Shuffle-Once, which was also considered (alongside random reshuffling) in Mishchenko et al. (2020). The authors also provide promising experiments in the federated setting. 

That said, the results of this paper, particularly in handling the random reshuffling difficulties, are somewhat of an incremental extension when compared to Mishchenko et al. (2020). It would be recommended that the authors better elaborate in what ways their results go beyond simply combining Mishchenko et al. (2020) with the standard prox analysis.


==== Minor comments ====

The authors should refer to, and compare with, the method FedAc [1].

Line 263: The equation is overlapping the text.

The authors note there is no tuning for the ProxRR vs SGD experiments (though tuning here would be *strongly* recommended), but what about for the federated optimization experiments? Please elaborate.


[1] Honglin Yuan, and Tengyu Ma. "Federated Accelerated Stochastic Gradient Descent." In NeurIPS. 2020.

In this paper, the authors propose two new algorithms for finite sum minimization: Proximal Random Reshuffling (ProxRR) and Federated Random Reshuffling (FedRR). ProxRR is used to solve composite functions, while FedRR is a special case of ProxRR as used in the context of federated optimization. They provide convergence rates under various convexity assumptions, and they also show convergence to small gradient norm (in expectation) for smooth non-convex objectives.

Main Review:
In extending random reshuffling methods to composite optimization, the authors observe the importance of only applying the proximal operator once every epoch of random reshuffling, rather than at the end of each step. Thus, their algorithm only evaluates the proximal operator a single time during each pass of the data. They go on to show that their results apply to the setting of Shuffle-Once, which was also considered (alongside random reshuffling) in Mishchenko et al. (2020). The authors also provide promising experiments in the federated setting. 

That said, the results of this paper, particularly in handling the random reshuffling difficulties, are somewhat of an incremental extension when compared to Mishchenko et al. (2020). It would be recommended that the authors better elaborate in what ways their results go beyond simply combining Mishchenko et al. (2020) with the standard prox analysis.


==== Minor comments ====

The authors should refer to, and compare with, the method FedAc [1].

Line 263: The equation is overlapping the text.

The authors note there is no tuning for the ProxRR vs SGD experiments (though tuning here would be *strongly* recommended), but what about for the federated optimization experiments? Please elaborate.


[1] Honglin Yuan, and Tengyu Ma. "Federated Accelerated Stochastic Gradient Descent." In NeurIPS. 2020.

Yes.

# Peer Review: ProxRR and FedRR Algorithms for Composite Finite-Sum Minimization

## Summary

This paper proposes ProxRR and FedRR algorithms for solving composite finite-sum minimization problems in both centralized and federated settings. The main contribution is a novel observation that the proximal operator should be applied once per epoch of random reshuffling rather than after each iteration. While the work provides convergence guarantees under various convexity assumptions and demonstrates promising experimental results, the theoretical contributions appear to be relatively straightforward extensions of prior work (Mishchenko et al. 2020) combined with standard proximal analysis techniques.

## Strengths

1. **Clear algorithmic insight**: The observation that applying the proximal operator once per epoch rather than at each step reduces computational complexity by a factor of n is intuitive and practically valuable. This design choice is well-motivated and leads to computational savings in proximal operator evaluations.

2. **Comprehensive convergence analysis**: The paper provides convergence rates across multiple settings—strongly convex losses, strongly convex regularizers, and nonconvex objectives—demonstrating the breadth of the theoretical results. The analysis covers both Random Reshuffling and Shuffle-Once variants.

3. **Solid theoretical foundation**: The paper includes complete proofs and a rigorous mathematical treatment, with proper characterization of convergence rates in terms of problem-dependent quantities like the shuffling radius.

4. **Promising experimental validation**: The federated learning experiments show practical benefits, suggesting the proposed methods have real-world applicability beyond the theoretical guarantees.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Limited novelty in theoretical methodology**: The core contribution appears to be a relatively direct combination of the random reshuffling framework from Mishchenko et al. (2020) with standard proximal gradient analysis. The paper does not sufficiently articulate what novel theoretical insights or techniques are required beyond this combination, which significantly limits the contribution's novelty.

2. **Insufficient clarity on conceptual advances**: While the algorithmic modification is clear, the paper lacks explicit discussion of what theoretical challenges arise when extending Mishchenko et al. (2020) to the proximal setting and how these are addressed. This makes it difficult to assess the true intellectual contribution beyond a straightforward extension.

3. **Incomplete experimental comparisons**: The federated optimization experiments omit comparison with the related method FedAc (Yuan and Ma, 2020), which is a notable gap given the federated learning focus. Including this comparison would strengthen claims about the practical advantages of the proposed approach.

4. **Inconsistent experimental methodology**: The ProxRR vs. SGD experiments explicitly note that no hyperparameter tuning was performed, which is acknowledged as suboptimal. However, the federated optimization experiments lack corresponding details about hyperparameter selection methodology, creating inconsistency in experimental rigor across different sections.

## Minor Issues

1. **Formatting error**: There is a text overlap with an equation at line 263 that should be corrected.

2. **Missing implementation details**: The federated optimization experiments would benefit from explicit documentation of hyperparameter tuning procedures, similar to the transparency provided for other experimental comparisons.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. **Strengthen novelty justification**: Provide a detailed discussion of the specific theoretical challenges encountered when extending Mishchenko et al. (2020) to the proximal setting. Clearly delineate what goes beyond simply combining existing techniques and highlight any non-obvious insights required for the extension.

2. **Include FedAc comparison**: Add experimental comparisons with the FedAc method in the federated optimization section to provide a more complete empirical evaluation relative to related work.

3. **Standardize experimental reporting**: Provide explicit details on hyperparameter tuning methodology for the federated experiments to match the level of transparency in other experimental sections.

4. **Fix formatting issues**: Correct the equation overlap at line 263.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a useful algorithmic contribution with solid theoretical analysis and promising experimental results. The core idea of applying the proximal operator once per epoch is practical and reduces computational complexity. However, the work is primarily an incremental extension of Mishchenko et al. (2020) to the proximal setting, combining it with standard proximal gradient analysis techniques. The limited novelty in theoretical methodology and insufficient articulation of what goes beyond straightforward extension of prior work place this contribution below the acceptance threshold for a top-tier venue. With revisions addressing the novelty concerns and completing the experimental comparisons, this work could potentially reach acceptance quality.

**Recommendation: Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold (5)**

The paper would benefit from major revisions clarifying its novel theoretical contributions and completing its experimental evaluation before resubmission.

reviews.summary: This paper first proposes a proximal variant of the gradient method with shuffling strategy for a class of composite finite-sum minimization problems, and then specifies it for an optimization problem in federated learning (FL). In fact, the proposed algorithm performs n gradient descent updates per epoch and then applies the proximal operator at the end of each epoch. Both single shuffling and randomized reshuffling strategies can be used. The authors analyze the convergence rates of this algorithm under the strong convexity of the losses and the strong convexity of the regularizer. Next, they apply the proposed algorithm to FL, with and without regularizer. This variant is similar to FedAvg, but it replaces SGD by a shuffling gradient scheme for local updates. They establish the convergence of this variant for both the strongly convex case (where the regularizer is strongly convex) and the non-convex case in both heterogeneity and homogeneity settings, respectively. Numerical experiments are conducted to illustrate the algorithm and compare it with two existing methods: local SGD and SCAFFOLD.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: The first part of this paper extends the results in [Mishchenko et al, 2020] to the composite setting. The convergence results are very similar to those in this work. The use of proximal operator at the end of each epoch makes sense since the convergence guarantee will be in epoch, and it is independent of the shuffling strategy. This is perhaps one of the main contributions. Algorithm 3 is very similar to FedAvg by replacing a classical SGD update with a shuffling gradient scheme. In this algorithm, I do not see where the proximal operator of R comes in. I believe that it should be placed after line 8. Overall, the originality of this paper is moderate. Except for adding a new proximal step, the results are very similar to [Mishchenko et al, 2020]. In fact, [Mishchenko et al, 2020] essentially extends and improves [Nguyen et al 2020] to randomized reshuffling variants.

Quality/clarity: The paper is well written and well-motivated in general. The extension to composite form is nontrivial as they have discussed. However, I have a feeling that the analysis for the strongly convex case is not much different from [Mishchenko et al, 2020]. Therefore, the quality of the paper is moderate. 
 
Significance: Overall, the paper has considerable contributions in terms of new algorithms, especially Algorithm 1, and the theoretical results associated with both Algorithms 1 and 3. But as mentioned above, due to the similarity of this paper and [Mishchenko et al, 2020], its significance is weak. 

Below are some additional comments:

-- The authors only consider the strongly convex case for Algorithm 1, but not the convex and nonconvex cases. Why are the two latter cases not considered? Is the latter case a special case of Theorem 5?

-- In Theorem 2, how can one estimate or upper bound sigma_rad to form a learning rate? In Theorem 1, its upper bound depends on x*? I believe that this is for theoretical guarantee, but it seems to be very small since it depends on both sigma_rad and epsilon (a desired accuracy). Could you please comment on this aspect?

-- Is there any difference in terms of convergence if either the regularizer is strongly convex or at least one loss is strongly convex?

-- Is Theorem 4 a special case of Theorem 3?

-- In terms of analysis, what is the essential difference between [Mishchenko et al, 2020] and this paper when a regularizer is added?
Without a regularizer, does this analysis reduce to [Mishchenko et al, 2020] or other previous works?

-- The proof of Lemma 7 is unclear to me, making me unable to verify Algorithm 3. Could you please add more details to clarify its proof?

-- In Supp. Doc. the authors present the convergence for diminishing learning rates, when t > t_0 ~=T/2. Can you use diminishing learning rate from t=1? If so, then how does the convergence rate in Theorem 9 change?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
Not discussed. 

--- I would suggest the authors to compare also single shuffling (like incremental) and randomized reshuffling in Supp. Doc.

Next, they apply the proposed algorithm to FL, with and without regularizer. This variant is similar to FedAvg, but it replaces SGD by a shuffling gradient scheme for local updates. Main Review:

In fact, [Mishchenko et al, 2020] essentially extends and improves [Nguyen et al 2020] to randomized reshuffling variants.

Quality/clarity: The paper is well written and well-motivated in general. The extension to composite form is nontrivial as they have discussed. However, I have a feeling that the analysis for the strongly convex case is not much different from [Mishchenko et al, 2020]. Therefore, the quality of the paper is moderate.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: ProxRR and FedRR Algorithms for Composite Finite-Sum Minimization

## Summary

This paper proposes ProxRR and FedRR, extending the random reshuffling framework to composite finite-sum minimization problems with proximal operators. The authors provide theoretical convergence guarantees for both centralized and federated settings, along with numerical experiments. While the work presents technically sound contributions and is generally well-written, the originality and significance are limited due to substantial overlap with prior work (Mishchenko et al., 2020), and the theoretical contributions are largely incremental in nature.

## Strengths

1. **Clear Presentation and Motivation**: The paper is well-written with good motivation for extending random reshuffling to the composite optimization setting. The problem formulation and algorithmic approach are clearly presented.

2. **Sensible Algorithmic Design**: The key idea of applying the proximal operator only once per epoch rather than after each iteration is intuitive and represents a meaningful algorithmic contribution. This design choice reduces computational overhead while maintaining convergence guarantees.

3. **Comprehensive Algorithm Development**: Algorithm 1 (ProxRR) and the federated variant (Algorithm 3, FedRR) represent considerable algorithmic contributions, with clear pseudocode and implementation details.

4. **Theoretical Analysis**: The paper provides convergence guarantees for both the centralized and federated algorithms under various settings, with detailed proofs. The extension to the federated learning context is a useful practical contribution.

5. **Empirical Validation**: Numerical experiments comparing against relevant baselines (Local SGD and SCAFFOLD) are provided, demonstrating practical performance.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Limited Originality**: The extension to composite form is substantially similar to Mishchenko et al. (2020), with the primary difference being the addition of a proximal step. The paper does not clearly articulate what is fundamentally novel in the analysis beyond adding a regularizer. When the regularizer is absent, the results should reduce to Mishchenko et al. (2020), but this connection is not explicitly verified or discussed.

2. **Narrow Scope of Theoretical Analysis**: For Algorithm 1 (ProxRR), only the strongly convex case is analyzed in the main results. The paper does not provide convergence analysis for convex or nonconvex cases, nor does it clarify whether these are special cases of Theorem 5 or require separate analysis. This significantly limits the applicability of the theoretical framework.

3. **Insufficient Differentiation of Strong Convexity Assumptions**: The paper does not clearly distinguish between scenarios where the regularizer ψ is strongly convex versus where individual losses f_i are strongly convex. The practical and theoretical implications of these different assumptions need clarification.

4. **Practical Applicability of Bounds**: Theorem 1 shows that the shuffling radius σ²_rad depends on ||∇f(x*)||, which involves the unknown optimum x*. The paper provides no guidance on how to estimate or upper bound σ_rad in practice to set the learning rate, severely limiting the practical utility of the theoretical results.

5. **Incomplete Algorithm Specification**: In Algorithm 3 (FedRR), it is unclear where the proximal operator of R is applied. Based on the analysis, it should appear after line 8, but this is not explicitly stated. This ambiguity undermines the clarity of the federated algorithm.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Relationship Between Results**: The relationship between Theorem 3 and Theorem 4 is unclear. It should be explicitly stated whether Theorem 4 is a special case of Theorem 3 or represents a distinct contribution.

2. **Incomplete Treatment of Decreasing Stepsize**: The analysis with diminishing learning rates is relegated to the supplementary material and only applies for t > t_0 ≈ T/2. The paper should discuss whether diminishing rates can be used from the beginning and how this affects the convergence rate in Theorem 9.

3. **Missing Empirical Comparisons**: The main paper lacks a direct comparison between single shuffling (incremental) and randomized reshuffling, which would help demonstrate the practical benefit of the proposed approach.

4. **Insufficient Discussion of Limitations**: The paper does not discuss limitations of the work or potential societal impacts, which are important for a complete contribution.

## Technical Issues and Suggestions

1. **Analysis Comparison with Prior Work**: The paper should explicitly explain the essential technical differences in the analysis compared to Mishchenko et al. (2020) when a regularizer is added. This would clarify the actual novelty of the theoretical contributions.

2. **Lemma 7 Details**: The proof of Lemma 7, which addresses the proximal operator computation in Algorithm 3, needs more detailed exposition to clarify the technical steps.

3. **Learning Rate Selection**: Provide practical guidance on estimating σ_rad or derive high-probability bounds that do not depend on the unknown optimum. This is critical for practitioners using these algorithms.

4. **Convex and Nonconvex Analysis**: Either provide convergence analysis for Algorithm 1 in the convex and nonconvex settings, or clearly explain why only the strongly convex case is considered and discuss the gaps in coverage.

## Minor Comments

- The federated setting analysis (Theorems 4-6) appropriately accounts for heterogeneity through local variances, consistent with the federated optimization literature.
- The nonconvex convergence result (Theorem 5) provides communication complexity bounds, though the dependence on problem-dependent constants could be better discussed.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes incremental contributions to the random reshuffling literature by extending it to composite optimization with proximal operators. While the algorithmic ideas are sensible and the theoretical analysis covers several settings, the work suffers from limited originality relative to Mishchenko et al. (2020), incomplete theoretical coverage (missing convex and nonconvex cases for the main algorithm), and practical limitations in applying the theoretical bounds due to dependence on unknown problem parameters. The federated extension is useful but relatively straightforward. The paper would benefit from clearer articulation of novelty, more complete theoretical analysis, and practical guidance for algorithm implementation.

**Recommendation**: The paper is marginally below the acceptance threshold. While it contains solid technical work, the incremental nature of the contributions, limited originality relative to prior work, and gaps in theoretical coverage (particularly for convex and nonconvex cases) prevent it from making a sufficiently strong contribution for acceptance at a top venue. Addressing the theoretical gaps and clarifying the novel aspects of the analysis would strengthen the work considerably.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper first proposes a proximal variant of the gradient method with shuffling strategy for a class of composite finite-sum minimization problems, and then specifies it for an optimization problem in federated learning (FL). In fact, the proposed algorithm performs n gradient descent updates per epoch and then applies the proximal operator at the end of each epoch. Both single shuffling and randomized reshuffling strategies can be used. The authors analyze the convergence rates of this algorithm under the strong convexity of the losses and the strong convexity of the regularizer. Next, they apply the proposed algorithm to FL, with and without regularizer. This variant is similar to FedAvg, but it replaces SGD by a shuffling gradient scheme for local updates. They establish the convergence of this variant for both the strongly convex case (where the regularizer is strongly convex) and the non-convex case in both heterogeneity and homogeneity settings, respectively. Numerical experiments are conducted to illustrate the algorithm and compare it with two existing methods: local SGD and SCAFFOLD.

Main Review:
Originality: The first part of this paper extends the results in [Mishchenko et al, 2020] to the composite setting. The convergence results are very similar to those in this work. The use of proximal operator at the end of each epoch makes sense since the convergence guarantee will be in epoch, and it is independent of the shuffling strategy. This is perhaps one of the main contributions. Algorithm 3 is very similar to FedAvg by replacing a classical SGD update with a shuffling gradient scheme. In this algorithm, I do not see where the proximal operator of R comes in. I believe that it should be placed after line 8. Overall, the originality of this paper is moderate. Except for adding a new proximal step, the results are very similar to [Mishchenko et al, 2020]. In fact, [Mishchenko et al, 2020] essentially extends and improves [Nguyen et al 2020] to randomized reshuffling variants.

Quality/clarity: The paper is well written and well-motivated in general. The extension to composite form is nontrivial as they have discussed. However, I have a feeling that the analysis for the strongly convex case is not much different from [Mishchenko et al, 2020]. Therefore, the quality of the paper is moderate. 
 
Significance: Overall, the paper has considerable contributions in terms of new algorithms, especially Algorithm 1, and the theoretical results associated with both Algorithms 1 and 3. But as mentioned above, due to the similarity of this paper and [Mishchenko et al, 2020], its significance is weak. 

Below are some additional comments:

-- The authors only consider the strongly convex case for Algorithm 1, but not the convex and nonconvex cases. Why are the two latter cases not considered? Is the latter case a special case of Theorem 5?

-- In Theorem 2, how can one estimate or upper bound sigma_rad to form a learning rate? In Theorem 1, its upper bound depends on x*? I believe that this is for theoretical guarantee, but it seems to be very small since it depends on both sigma_rad and epsilon (a desired accuracy). Could you please comment on this aspect?

-- Is there any difference in terms of convergence if either the regularizer is strongly convex or at least one loss is strongly convex?

-- Is Theorem 4 a special case of Theorem 3?

-- In terms of analysis, what is the essential difference between [Mishchenko et al, 2020] and this paper when a regularizer is added?
Without a regularizer, does this analysis reduce to [Mishchenko et al, 2020] or other previous works?

-- The proof of Lemma 7 is unclear to me, making me unable to verify Algorithm 3. Could you please add more details to clarify its proof?

-- In Supp. Doc. the authors present the convergence for diminishing learning rates, when t > t_0 ~=T/2. Can you use diminishing learning rate from t=1? If so, then how does the convergence rate in Theorem 9 change?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Not discussed. 

--- I would suggest the authors to compare also single shuffling (like incremental) and randomized reshuffling in Supp. Doc.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The paper proposes ProxRR and FedRR algorithms extending random reshuffling to composite finite-sum minimization with proximal operators. While the work presents new algorithms and theoretical results, the originality is moderate due to significant similarity to prior work (Mishchenko et al., 2020), and the significance is weakened by the incremental nature of the contributions.
strengths: Well-written paper with good motivation for the extension to composite form
The key idea of applying the proximal operator only at the end of each epoch (rather than after each iteration) is sensible and represents a main contribution
Considerable contributions in terms of new algorithms, especially Algorithm 1 (ProxRR)
Theoretical results are provided for both Algorithms 1 and 3
The extension to federated learning (FedRR) is a useful application
Numerical experiments are conducted comparing against existing methods (Local SGD and SCAFFOLD)
weaknesses: Limited originality - the extension to composite form is similar to Mishchenko et al. (2020), with the main difference being the addition of a proximal step
Analysis for the strongly convex case appears not much different from Mishchenko et al. (2020)
Only strongly convex case is analyzed for Algorithm 1; convex and nonconvex cases are not considered
In Algorithm 3 (FedRR), it is unclear where the proximal operator of R is applied - should be placed after line 8
Weak overall significance due to similarity to prior work (Mishchenko et al., 2020)
Limitations and societal impact are not discussed
No comparison between single shuffling (incremental) and randomized reshuffling in main paper
suggestions: Provide analysis for convex and nonconvex cases for Algorithm 1, or clarify if these are special cases of Theorem 5
Discuss how to estimate or upper bound σ_rad in practice to form a learning rate, since Theorem 1 shows dependence on x* (the optimum)
Clarify whether there is a practical difference in convergence between regularizer being strongly convex versus at least one loss being strongly convex
Clarify if Theorem 4 is a special case of Theorem 3
Explain the essential difference in analysis between this paper and Mishchenko et al. (2020) when a regularizer is added, and verify that results reduce to prior work when regularizer is absent
Add more details to the proof of Lemma 7 to clarify the proximal operator computation in Algorithm 3
Address the question of whether diminishing learning rates can be used from t=1 (rather than t > t_0 ≈ T/2) and how convergence rate in Theorem 9 would change
Include comparison between single shuffling and randomized reshuffling in the main document
Discuss limitations and potential societal impacts of the work
main_points: The paper extends random reshuffling to composite finite-sum problems with proximal operators, but the core analysis is very similar to Mishchenko et al. (2020)
The main novelty is applying the proximal operator once per epoch rather than per iteration, which is intuitive but represents an incremental contribution
Algorithm 3 (FedRR) is similar to FedAvg with local random reshuffling instead of SGD, but lacks clarity on proximal operator placement
Theoretical analysis covers only strongly convex cases for the main algorithm, leaving convex and nonconvex cases unaddressed
The practical applicability of the bounds depends on σ_rad, which depends on the unknown optimum x*, limiting practical utility
The relationship between strong convexity of regularizer versus strong convexity of losses is not clearly differentiated
The essential technical contributions beyond Mishchenko et al. (2020) are not clearly articulated
technical_details: Theorem 2 provides linear convergence with constant stepsize when each fi is μ-strongly convex, but convergence to neighborhood size proportional to γ²σ²_rad
Theorem 1 bounds shufﬂing radius: σ²_rad ≤ L_max/2 * n(n||∇f(x*)||² + 1/2 σ²_*), where σ²_* is population variance at optimum
Theorem 3 covers case where ψ is μ-strongly convex but f_i are only convex, with exponential convergence rate (1 + 2γμn)^(-T)
The shufﬂing radius σ²_rad depends on problem constants and ||∇f(x*)||, making it difficult to estimate in advance
In federated setting, σ²_rad depends on sum of local variances and local gradient norms, consistent with Local GD/SGD literature
Theorem 4 provides convergence guarantee for heterogeneous federated setting with strongly convex regularizer
Theorem 5 provides nonconvex convergence with communication complexity O(1/ε² + σ√n/ε³ + ζ/ε³)
Theorem 6 shows convergence improves with number of devices M in denominator for i.i.d. data case
Decreasing stepsize analysis is relegated to supplementary material with diminishing rates applied only when t > t_0 ≈ T/2
